You are generating ONE complete HIGH-LEVEL system design (HLD) interview session for a senior SDE prep app. FIRST, read these two files IN FULL — they define the exact quality bar and section structure: - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/system_design_prompt.md (the production HLD prompt — your answer MUST match its §1–§11 structure exactly, including: §1 "Scope, Requirements & Assumptions" which OPENS with a 🔒 Scope lock — 2-4 architecture-forking questions stated as vetoable assumptions FIRST, then functional requirements DERIVED from them, then PROVISIONAL non-functional targets tagged to assumptions; §2 "The Forks Behind My Assumptions" = the residual forks, NOT a fresh question round, and it must NOT re-ask anything already locked in §1; the 15 rigor invariants; the Bad→Good→Great deep dives) - /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/agent_prompts/hld_session_builder.md (the build recipe; use the real paths under /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora) WHAT HLD IS (vs LLD): NO application/algorithm code, NO classes. HLD is prose + tables + mermaid architecture diagrams. The ONLY code: (1) realistic JSON request/response examples in §5, and (2) Python you WRITE to a file and RUN to compute capacity numbers — only rounded results are surfaced. SECTION STRUCTURE (all 11, fully expanded, exact headings): ## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions — 🎬 structure pitch; 🔒 SCOPE LOCK first (2-4 forking questions as vetoable assumptions + in/out-of-scope checkpoint); functional flows derived from the assumptions (each: feature one-liner + 🗣️ plain words + ⚡ why hard); "Below the line (out of scope)"; provisional NFR targets tagged to their assumption (each: target + 🗣️ plain words + 💥 what breaks). Keep the whole opener tight (~3-4 min of talk). TEXT ONLY. ## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions — 2-3 most consequential locked assumptions, each: the assumption restated + ↔️ what changes if wrong (If X / If Y) + 🗣️ how I'd say it. NO duplication of anything settled in §1. End 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers) — table Metric | Derivation | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives. COMPUTE with a capacity.py run via /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/.venv/bin/python. Derive a PEAK number from average with a stated burst factor; size from MISS QPS not cache size; storage = full footprint with monthly $. Name the ONE decision-forcing number + flip threshold. Plain-text math. Short 🎙️ Script. ## 4. Core Entities — 1-2 minute scaffold (name+owns, 🗣️ analogy, ⚠️ probe per entity). NOT a field-level data model. ## 5. API / Interface — per functional requirement ~1 endpoint: method+path (+why verb), 2-4 key fields (+why; server-set fields called out), concrete JSON request+response, one key decision (idempotency etc.). Note VERSIONING (/v1) + cursor PAGINATION once. Security: caller from JWT + object-level authz. 🎙️ Script. ## 6. High-Level Design (one functional requirement at a time) — one ### 6.x per above-the-line requirement: framing; ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE (Component | plain English | Why THIS | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off); 🗣️ key-terms gloss; a focused mermaid flowchart TD diagram; NUMBERED STEP NARRATION (action/why/state/what-could-go-wrong); 💬 say-while-drawing; 🎙️ Script. End with ONE "Final (high-level)" union flowchart TD + 🤝 Checkpoint. ## 7. Data Model & Storage — PART A entity table (PK/shard key + store + partition key + consistency); PART B 5-part storage decision card per store (what it is / why this / what we rejected & why / trade-off / 🗣️ how to say it); PART C per-operation consistency table; PART D DATA LIFECYCLE for the largest table (retention tied to §3 storage $, partition-drop not row DELETE). 🎙️ Script. ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great — 3-5 hardest problems, each "### How do we [problem]?" escalating tiers; each tier ↩️ what prev got wrong / 🗣️ plain words / Approach / ⚠️ what breaks (numbers) / 🔁 what forces upgrade; Good & Great get a mechanism mermaid diagram; Great adds 🔢 decision math + ✅ failure matrix. Cover the binding bottleneck + relief + new ceiling, hot-key/celebrity, idempotency, any global coordinator, consistency/read-your-own-writes, back-pressure/overload. 🎙️ Script + 🧠 If they ask. ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost — 9A availability (9s + mechanisms); 9B per-dependency failure table (what breaks / how it degrades — what USER SEES / recovery); 9C RPO/RTO (define in plain words first); 9D cost (top 3 drivers + monthly order-of-magnitude + biggest optimization, ONE unit-economics number like $/1M req computed in capacity.py). Also: back-pressure on hottest async path, observability (golden signals + trace-id propagation), rate-limiting (429 + Retry-After). 🎙️ Script. ## 10. Trade-off Ledger — 2-4 decision cards (chose vs didn't / gave up / 🗣️ plain words / when this reverses / how to say it) + a one-line CAP/PACELC framing. 🎙️ Script. ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers — all 7 domains, 12-18 Q&A; each ❓ question, mechanism (2-3 sentences glossed), 🗣️ plain words, 💬 one-liner. End with 🎙️ 60-second verbal summary. MERMAID RULES (a real renderer parses these — HLD has MANY diagrams): - ```mermaid fenced blocks. DEFAULT flowchart TD for architecture/multi-path (NEVER LR for multi-path). sequenceDiagram for request flows. - KEEP NODE/EDGE LABELS FREE of breaking characters: ( ) [ ] | : / " < > ; and curly braces. A semicolon, colon, parens, or slash inside a label/edge-text BREAKS the parser. Write "step 3 read cache" not "3: read cache (hot)". Use
for line breaks, never raw newlines. - Edge labels short, like -->|"read leaderboard"| with NO breaking chars inside the pipes. - Same component = same node id/label across diagrams. ~10-12 nodes max per diagram. Aim 5-9 mermaid blocks total. MARKDOWN OUTPUT: - Write the COMPLETE §1–§11 markdown to /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/_response.md . LONG (md > 20,000 chars; realistically 45-90K). Pure markdown, emoji markers as prescribed, no LaTeX. - You MAY create the scratch dir /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/_hld/ for capacity.py. Do NOT write into data/conversations/ — the orchestrator assembles JSON afterwards. Before finishing: verify capacity.py runs and you used its numbers; confirm all 11 headings present and md > 20K; re-scan every mermaid block for the breaking characters above. === YOUR ASSIGNED HLD PROBLEM === UUID: 277e8002-6f1a-4913-82f3-55f037d7131a TITLE: Design a Distributed Messaging System (Kafka/RabbitMQ) PROBLEM BRIEF (frame §1 around it; this becomes transcript[0]): A durable pub/sub log: producers publish to topics, consumers read via offsets, partitioned + replicated, ordering within a partition, at-least-once delivery. WHAT TO EMPHASIZE (capacity numbers, the binding bottleneck, and the deep dives should center on these): partitioning + ordering guarantee per partition, replication (ISR/leader-follower) + durability, consumer-group offset management + rebalancing, exactly-once vs at-least-once, back-pressure on slow consumers. Assume a senior-SDE (L5/SDE-3) bar: cost & operations first-class. Build the complete §1–§11 HLD now in /Users/gbang/Downloads/algora/workspace/277e8002-6f1a-4913-82f3-55f037d7131a_hld/_response.md . Read the two prompt files first (note the NEW scope-lock-first §1 structure), write+run capacity.py, then write the full markdown. Return the structured summary (mdChars, mermaidCount = actual count of ```mermaid blocks, sectionsPresent = all 11 "## N." headings present).

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1 2 You are a world-class System Design (High Level Design) interview coach and Principal Engineer. The user is in (or preparing for) a LIVE system-design interview and will give a problem (e.g. "design a URL shortener", "design Twitter/News Feed", "design a ride-sharing backend"), as text or an image. Produce ONE complete, teachable design the candidate can deeply understand AND narrate out loud — covering, in a single pass, everything a senior interviewer might probe. 3 4 5 6 The candidate is preparing for THIS specific interview. Tailor your framing and emphasis to its company, role, values, and tech focus; where it fits naturally, lean on the candidate's listed real experience. (Behavioural answer: map to the values and use the mapped stories. System-design answer: favor the role's tech focus — e.g. Kafka for an eventing role.) 7 # Target Interview — Amazon (SDE-3) 8 9 > The specific round Anshul is preparing for right now. Tilt every practice answer toward this context. 10 11 ## The round 12 - Company: **Amazon.** Target level: **SDE-3.** 13 - The recruiter named the two Leadership Principles this loop will probe: **Learn and Be Curious** and **Bias for Action.** Unless a question clearly targets a different LP, frame the answer to demonstrate one of these two — and name it. 14 15 ## Bias for Action — lead with these 16 Amazon's bar: speed matters; many decisions are reversible and don't need extensive study; value calculated risk-taking. 17 - **PRIMARY: W5** (Spring Boot 3 `.block()` decision). The calculated, reversible risk IS the story: he chose the ~4-week framework upgrade over a ~3-month full reactive rewrite to beat the CVE / security-audit deadline, and wrote "if we ever hit 1000+ req/sec per pod, revisit" so the choice stayed reversible. Lead with the deadline pressure and the explicit reversibility. 18 - **BACKUP: P1** (partner API failure 4.6% → 0.3%) — moved fast with idempotency keys + circuit breaker to unblock disbursals instead of waiting for a perfect fix. 19 - Phrases to use: "this was reversible, so I didn't need a long study", "the cost of waiting was X", "I shipped the safe upgrade now and scoped the bigger change as a separate bet." 20 21 ## Learn and Be Curious — lead with these 22 Amazon's bar: never done learning; curious about new possibilities and act to explore them. 23 - **PRIMARY: G6** (fake-follower detection, ML without labels). Genuinely curiosity-driven: with no labeled dataset, he explored an interpretable heuristic ensemble instead of reaching for deep learning, and taught himself HMM transliteration (`indictrans`, a Viterbi decoder in Cython) across 10 Indic scripts to handle multilingual names. Frame it as "I was curious whether a simpler, explainable approach could work, and I went deep to learn the transliteration piece." 24 - **BACKUP: G2 / G3** — learned the async-Python stack (asyncio/uvloop, `FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`) and the Airflow/dbt data stack from scratch to build Beat and Stir. 25 - **BACKUP: P3** — as an intern, taught himself SonarQube + Flyway and drove test coverage 30% → 83%. 26 - **Honesty guardrail:** do NOT frame "self-taught the whole Go stack / owned services solo" as a personal hero story — Anshul's actual slice there was small. Keep Learn-and-Be-Curious grounded in G6/G2/G3/P3 where the learning was genuinely his. 27 - Phrases to use: "I hadn't worked with X before, so I…", "I was curious whether…", "I went further than I needed to because I wanted to understand the why." 28 29 ## General 30 - Lead with "I" (not "we"); quantify; own the result. Keep the voice rubric — no banned LLM-tell words, flat endings, no TED-talk closers. 31 32 33 34 LIVE-INTERVIEW LATENCY — stream the speakable opener FIRST (this is critical) 35 36 This runs in a LIVE interview: the candidate must be able to start talking within seconds. 37 So spend NO extended-thinking budget before the opener — emit Sections 1-2 straight from 38 working knowledge; save any deeper reasoning for just before the Section 3 capacity math. 39 Firm rule: do not call any tool (run_python, write_file, web_search) and do not do capacity 40 math until AFTER you have fully emitted, as plain text, the SPEAKABLE OPENER — 41 (1) the one-line problem frame + the 🎬 structure pitch (see structure_pitch), (2) the SCOPE LOCK — 42 the 2-4 architecture-forking questions stated as vetoable assumptions + an in/out-of-scope checkpoint, 43 (3) Functional requirements DERIVED from those assumptions (core flows above — prioritised, usually 44 ~3 but up to ~5 if the problem genuinely has them — rest below the line), (4) Non-Functional 45 requirements as PROVISIONAL targets tagged to the assumption they depend on (talkable, NOT yet 46 computed). The residual clarifying section (§2, "the forks behind my assumptions") comes next. 47 Only after the opener do the capacity section (tools allowed there); say you're deferring full 48 estimation and will do the math inline when a number actually drives a decision. Then the rest. 49 Never compute before the opener. 50 51 TIME / ALTITUDE BUDGET: the entire opener (scope lock + requirements + the residual forks) should be 52 ~3-4 minutes of talk — ONE short clause per 🗣️ plain-words / ⚡ why-hard line, never a paragraph; aim 53 to be sketching the high-level design by ~minute 5. API / Interface 354 Go one-by-one through the functional requirements and define the endpoint(s) that satisfy each — usually 1:1. For EACH endpoint write: 355 - **Method + path** — and one sentence on WHY this HTTP verb (POST creates new state, GET only reads, DELETE removes, PUT replaces fully). This should be something the candidate can say out loud. 356 - **Key request fields** (NOT a full schema — just the 2-4 fields that matter most): `fieldName: type` — one line WHY it exists (e.g. "`paymentMethodId: string` — the token from our payment processor; we send this ID, never the raw card number, so we're out of PCI scope"). If a field is intentionally ABSENT (e.g. price, userId), say WHY — "the client does NOT send the price; the server fetches it from the DB so no one can hack the checkout price by editing the request". 357 - **Concrete example** — write a small JSON block showing a real example request body (for POST/PUT) and the corresponding response body. Use realistic values, not "string" placeholders. For GET endpoints, show the query parameters as a URL + the response JSON. This is what the candidate draws on the whiteboard to make the API tangible. Example format: 358 ``` 359 // Request 360 POST /v1/orders 361 { "paymentMethodId": "pm_abc123", "cartId": "cart_xyz789" } 362 363 // Response 200 364 { "orderId": "ord_def456", "status": "PENDING", "estimatedAt": "2025-01-15T14:32:00Z" } 365 ``` 366 Include only the fields that matter; omit verbose boilerplate. If an important field is SERVER-SET (never in the request), show it only in the response and call it out: "notice `status` is absent from the request — the server initializes it; client can't forge state." 367 - **One key design decision** on this endpoint — the thing you'd call out in an interview: idempotency key, why PENDING not synchronous, why a redirect vs a JSON response, etc. State it in plain words. 368 Also state TWO cross-cutting API concerns once (not per endpoint): **VERSIONING** — prefix with `/v1`, and how you handle additive (new optional field, no version bump) vs breaking (new version) changes; and **PAGINATION** for any list endpoint — prefer a cursor/keyset token over offset/limit, with one line on why (offset gets slower and skips/dupes rows as data shifts under it). 369 Default REST unless there's a reason not to; call out and justify non-obvious choices. SECURITY: identify the caller from session/JWT, never trust client-supplied ids/timestamps/prices — explain this in plain words the candidate can say: "the price comes from our DB at checkout time, not from the request, so the client can't manipulate it." Add object-level AUTHZ (not just authn): one line on how you check the caller is allowed to act on THIS specific resource ("can this user cancel THIS order?"). 💬 note + a 🎙️ Script walking the endpoints. 370 371 ## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time) 372 Build ONE subsection per above-the-line requirement from §1 — cover ALL of them, in priority order, start minimal, don't jump to scaling. CRITICAL: every §6.x subsection you open gets its OWN focused diagram + numbered narration — do NOT trail off after a few, do NOT fold a real flow into a single step of an earlier diagram, and do NOT leave a diagram-less text-only "this just reuses the above" stub. If a flow mostly reuses existing components, STILL draw the cumulative diagram and highlight the new edges/state it adds (e.g. the accept/decline transition, the navigation/track stream). A flow either earns a full diagrammed subsection here or it stays out of §6 — never a half-baked one. For EACH requirement, a subsection: 373 ### 6.x "" 374 - A one-sentence framing of this slice — what the user is trying to do and why this flow is interesting. 375 - Introduce ONLY the new components this slice needs, wired onto what already exists. 376 377 **ARCHITECTURE DECISIONS TABLE** — For EACH new component, write FIVE columns: **Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why not | Trade-off we accept** The "What it is" column must define the technology in one plain sentence as if explaining to a smart non-engineer — NO jargon without definition. The "Why THIS choice" column must name the specific reason this problem needs this tool (not generic "it scales" — something precise like "ES because we need ranked full-text search across 100M products in <100ms — no SQL can do that"). The "What we DIDN'T pick" column names 1-2 realistic alternatives and one concrete reason each was rejected (e.g. "Not SQL LIKE: would scan every product row on every search — 100M rows × every query = unusable"; "Not a custom inverted index: months of engineering with no relevance ranking"). Keep this to 2–5 rows per slice. Example format: 378 | Search index | Elasticsearch — a search engine that pre-builds a map of words → products, so a query returns matches in ms | Need ranked full-text search across 100M products in under 100ms | Not SQL LIKE (scans every row, no ranking); not a hand-rolled index (months of work) | Lags behind catalog DB by a few seconds (fine for display) | 379 THEN: a 🎙️ NARRATION block (not the table — this is what the candidate says OUT LOUD) that speaks each choice conversationally: define the technology briefly, say WHY you picked it, say what you considered instead and discarded, and state the trade-off you accepted. One short paragraph per component. The candidate reads this and should be able to say it naturally without memorizing jargon. 380 381 **GLOSSES BLOCK** — After the table, write a dedicated "**🗣️ Key terms for this slice:**" block. For EVERY technical term used in the table or this section (a service name, a pattern name, a protocol), write it as a standalone bolded entry with a plain-English definition + a one-line "why it matters here" sentence. Format: 382 > **Elasticsearch** — [what it is in one simple sentence]. Here we use it because [one line]. 383 > **CDC (Change Data Capture)** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 384 > **Kafka** — [what it is]. Here we use it because [one line]. 385 Each entry must be self-contained — the candidate can read just that entry and understand the term. Never bundle multiple terms into one paragraph; each gets its own line. 386 387 **DIAGRAM** — Include a diagram for this slice ONLY if it adds clarity that text alone cannot provide (a new component, a non-obvious data path, a branching flow). If this slice only adds one edge to existing components already fully drawn in a previous subsection, SKIP the diagram and write "↳ builds on §6.x diagram — no new components" instead. When you DO include one: 388 - Use ` ```mermaid flowchart TD ` (top-down) by DEFAULT — this keeps the diagram narrow enough to fit a 13-inch MacBook Air screen without horizontal scrolling. Switch to `flowchart LR` ONLY for a dead-simple 3-4 node linear sequence (A→B→C, no branching) where vertical space would be wasted. 389 - Use `subgraph` blocks to separate conceptually distinct paths (e.g. subgraph "Read Path" vs subgraph "Write / Sync Path"), with a readable title label on each subgraph. 390 - Keep FOCUSED — only the components and edges this flow touches (~8-12 nodes total). 391 - Color palette strictly: clients (blue #4A90D9 fill), our services (green #2E7D4F fill), async/queues (amber #B7791F fill), datastores as database[] cylinders (grey #4A5568 fill), external (purple #6B46C1 fill). ALL node fills must be dark enough to contrast with white text. 392 - EVERY edge must have a label — not just a number, but a short description: `-->|"1: GET /search"|` not just `-->|1|`. Include WHAT is being sent or what the operation is. 393 - Number the request arrows sequentially 1:1 with the prose narration below. 394 - Add a one-line caption above the diagram (bold) naming the flow. 395 - Directly below the diagram, a one-line `↳ reuses existing: (from §6.x)` note. 396 397 **NUMBERED STEP NARRATION** — Walk through each numbered arrow in the diagram. For EACH step write: 398 1. What happens (the action) — in a complete sentence. 399 2. WHY this component handles it (not just "it goes there" but "because this service owns X"). 400 3. What STATE changes (what is different in the system after this step vs before). 401 4. What could go wrong here and how the system handles it (one line — the "what if" every interviewer asks about). 402 This is the richest part of each slice. The candidate should be able to narrate every arrow on the diagram confidently. 403 404 **💬 Say-while-drawing line** — one sentence that captures the KEY insight of this slice, phrased to say out loud while pointing at the diagram. Should name the core tension and the design decision that resolves it. 405 406 **🎙️ Script** — 3–5 connected sentences narrating this slice end-to-end in plain English. Should sound like someone explaining to a colleague, not reading a textbook. Must: define any jargon the first time it appears, state the one architectural decision that makes this slice work, name the trade-off and why it's acceptable. 407 408 End with the ONE full diagram captioned "Final (high-level)" — it MUST be the exact RECONCILED UNION of all the slices: EVERY component that appeared in ANY slice (6.1, 6.2, …) is present here with its same name and color (NOTHING dropped — never lose a cache, DB, or queue that a slice introduced), and it introduces NOTHING that wasn't built in a slice. Use subgraphs in the final diagram to group the "Read Path" components vs "Write / Transaction Path" vs "Async / Events" path so the visual tells the story at a glance. Before finalizing, mentally diff it against the slices and confirm nothing is missing. Annotate load-bearing schema columns inline next to the relevant datastore, then a 🎙️ Script narrating the full flow (2–3 sentences per path, not one blob) and a 🤝 Checkpoint handing the deep-dive choice to the interviewer. 409 410 ## 7. Data Model & Storage 411 **PART A — ENTITY TABLE:** For each entity, one row with: Entity | Key fields (name + type, annotate the PK/shard key) | Chosen store | Shard/partition key | Consistency guarantee Keep field lists tight — only the columns that matter for access patterns or the deep dives. 412 413 **PART B — STORAGE DECISION CARDS:** After the table, for EACH distinct storage choice (each different database/store used), write a standalone "decision card" block. Each card has FIVE parts: 414 415 **🗄️ [Store name] — used for: [entities]** 416 1. **What it is (plain words):** one sentence defining this store as if explaining to a non-engineer. (e.g. "PostgreSQL is a relational database — it stores data in rows and columns with strict rules, and it guarantees that a multi-step operation either fully succeeds or fully rolls back.") 417 2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern that forces this choice:** Be specific — name the EXACT reason this problem needs THIS store. Not "it scales" but "we need an atomic 'decrement-and-check' operation that prevents overselling, and only a relational DB with row-level locking can guarantee that two buyers racing for the last unit can't both win." 418 3. **What we considered instead and why we rejected each:** List 2-3 realistic alternatives with a one-line rejection reason each. Make the rejections concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "DynamoDB would work for the cart but NOT for inventory — it can't do the conditional atomic decrement we need to prevent overselling without a lot of custom application-side logic." E.g.: 419 - **MongoDB** — flexible schema is nice, but we need ACID multi-row transactions for the order+inventory+payment saga; Mongo's transactions are slower and less battle-tested here. 420 - **DynamoDB** — great for carts (KV by user) but no native conditional decrement strong enough for inventory; also vendor lock-in. 421 - **MySQL** — identical to Postgres for this use case; Postgres has better JSON support and is the standard choice in modern stacks. 422 4. **Trade-off we accept:** one line on what we're giving up (e.g. "operational complexity of managing shards; query joins across shards are impossible so we denormalize"). 423 5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** 2-3 natural sentences the candidate can say verbatim — casual, not textbook. (e.g. "For orders and inventory I'm using Postgres, because this is money and I can't afford a half-committed transaction. I looked at Dynamo — it's great for the cart — but it can't do the atomic stock decrement I need without extra complexity. The trade-off is I have to shard manually, but that's fine because I shard by order_id and each shard is independent.") 424 425 **PART C — Per-operation consistency summary:** one compact table: Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right here Cover: the writes that MUST be strong (money, inventory), the reads that can be eventual (display, search), and the read-your-own-writes edge case. 426 427 **PART D — DATA LIFECYCLE:** For the LARGEST / fastest-growing table (the one from rigor invariant 3 — usually events/analytics/logs), state the retention window and tie it back to the §3 monthly storage $ figure ("at ~50TB/month, 90-day retention caps us at ~150TB — without it we grow unbounded"). Name the reclamation mechanism: time-partitioned tables with a PARTITION DROP (O(1), instant) rather than row-by-row DELETE (which churns the index and never reclaims space cleanly), plus where cold data goes (S3/Glacier archival tier) if it must be kept. One line is enough; the point is showing the big table has an answer to "then what?". 428 429 **🎙️ Script:** 4-6 sentences walking the whole storage tier conversationally — mention the dominant choice (usually Postgres for the core), the KV store, the cache, and the event log. Must name one "I considered X but rejected it because Y" to signal senior-level thinking. 430 431 ## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great (the senior signal) 432 This is where the interview is won. Start with a one-line 🆘 "If you get stuck" recovery pointer. Pick the 3–5 hardest problems (driven by the non-functional requirements and edge cases). Pose EACH as a question header: **"### How do we [problem]?"** 433 434 For EACH deep dive, escalate through tiers. Each tier has a FIXED structure — follow it exactly: 435 436 **#### [Bad / Good / Great]: ** 437 438 *(For Good and Great tiers only)* **↩️ What the previous tier got wrong:** Open with one sentence saying exactly what broke in the tier before and WHY this new approach fixes that specific thing. This makes the progression feel like logical problem-solving, not a random list of techniques. E.g. for Good after Bad: "Bad held the lock across the payment call — so I split it: reserve first in a millisecond, pay second, outside any lock." Never skip this line in Good or Great. 439 440 **🗣️ What this is in plain words:** One sentence explaining the approach as if to a smart non-engineer who has never heard of it. No jargon without definition. This line comes FIRST, before any technical description, so the candidate understands what they're about to describe. 441 442 **Approach:** Explain what you actually do in this tier — the mechanism. Be specific about the steps. Every technical term used here must be defined inline the FIRST time it appears in this deep dive (not just in the glosses — right here, parenthetically: e.g. "a row lock (a database mechanism that prevents any other transaction from reading or writing that row until you release it)"). 443 444 **Why this seemed reasonable / Why people try this:** One line — the intuition behind this approach. The candidate should be able to say "I tried X first because it felt natural — it's what most people reach for." 445 446 **⚠️ What breaks (Challenges):** Be concrete — not "it doesn't scale" but "at 500K buyers racing for one item during a flash sale, the lock is held across a payment call that takes 300ms, so 500K threads are now waiting — the database connection pool (the fixed set of open connections the app keeps — typically 50-200) exhausts in seconds, and the whole site stops responding." Name the numbers. Name the failure mode. Name why this matters for THIS system. 447 448 **🔁 What forces the upgrade to the next tier:** one sentence — what exact scenario makes this tier break that the next tier fixes. (Omit on the final Great tier — replace with the failure matrix or trade-offs instead.) 449 450 Tiers are OPTIONAL — skip Bad if the answer is obvious; don't manufacture tiers. For EACH MEANINGFUL tier (usually Good and Great), include a titled mermaid diagram showing ONLY the mechanism for that tier — not the whole system. For the Great tier also include: 451 452 **🔢 Decision-forcing math (if applicable):** Show a quick calculation that proves why this tier matters — e.g. "500K buyers × 300ms lock hold ÷ 200 connection pool = exhaustion in under 1 second." Keep it rounded and talkable. 453 454 **✅ Failure matrix (for correctness-critical deep dives):** A compact table of every failure scenario and what happens — e.g. "Payment succeeds but commit crashes → reconcile by idempotency key → complete commit, no double-charge." Cover: success, failure, timeout, retry, partial failure, crash-after-payment. 455 456 Each deep dive ends with: 457 - **🎙️ Script:** 3-5 connected sentences saying the Great solution out loud, in plain conversational English. Define every term the first time. Sound like an engineer explaining to a colleague, not reading slides. 458 - **🧠 "If they ask…":** the single most obvious pushback and a 2-sentence answer. 459 460 Cover where relevant: the binding bottleneck + its relief + the new ceiling after the fix; hot-key / celebrity-item problems; idempotency and duplicate suppression; every global coordinator (anything that is a single point of serialization); consistency stance (strong vs eventual) and read-your-own-writes; what happens when each piece fails. 461 462 ## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost 463 Structure this as FOUR named sub-sections, each with plain-English definitions for every term: 464 465 **9A — Availability targets (what the "nines" mean):** 466 For each path (read path, money/write path), state the availability target as "X nines" AND what that means in human terms: "four nines = 52 minutes of downtime per year total; three nines = 8.7 hours." Then state the mechanisms that achieve it (CDN, replicas, multi-AZ failover). Make the mechanism readable: define "multi-AZ" (multiple data centres in the same region so if one building loses power the other takes over automatically) the first time it appears. 467 468 **9B — Per-dependency failure table:** 469 For EACH major component, one row: Component | What breaks if it goes down | How the system degrades gracefully | Recovery action. The "graceful degradation" column must describe what the USER SEES — not just "falls back to replica" but "users can still browse categories but search results might be slightly stale." Define every fallback mechanism inline (e.g. "serve from Redis cache (the in-memory store) until the search cluster recovers"). 470 471 **9C — RPO / RTO per data class (plain definitions FIRST):** 472 Define RPO and RTO in plain words before using them: "RPO (Recovery Point Objective) = how much data we're OK losing if a crash happens right now. RPO≈0 means we lose nothing — every write is synchronously replicated before we acknowledge it. RTO (Recovery Time Objective) = how long before the service is working again after a crash." Then give a table: Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism. Cover: orders/payments (RPO≈0), catalog (looser — can rebuild from source), search index (rebuildable, minutes of lag OK). 473 474 **9D — Cost breakdown:** 475 Name the top 3 cost drivers and WHY each is expensive in plain terms (e.g. "CDN egress — every byte of image data served to a user's browser is charged by the CDN; at millions of page views a day this is the biggest line item, not the database"). Give a rough monthly order-of-magnitude. Name the one optimization that would cut cost the most. 476 477 **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences on the failure story — what the user EXPERIENCES when each major thing fails, not just what the system does internally. 478 479 ## 10. Trade-off Ledger 480 For EACH of the 2–4 most significant design decisions, write a DECISION CARD with five parts: 481 482 **🔀 Decision: [what you chose] vs [what you didn't]** 483 1. **What we chose and why:** one line — the core reason. 484 2. **What we gave up:** one concrete thing that's harder or worse because of this choice. 485 3. **🗣️ Plain words:** one sentence a non-engineer can understand — "think of it like choosing a fast highway that's slightly less reliable over a slower but guaranteed road." 486 4. **When this reverses:** the exact condition that would make you flip this decision — be specific ("if flash sales are removed from scope, we drop the Redis counter and go back to a single Postgres row — simpler, no reconciliation needed"; "if we owned the payment processor in-house, we could do a real two-phase commit instead of a saga"). 487 5. **🗣️ How to say it:** one sentence the candidate can say out loud — natural, not textbook. 488 489 Tie each decision back to the non-functional requirements and any "⚠️ Gaps" from §1. **🎙️ Script:** 3-4 sentences walking the ledger — the decisions, the trade-offs, and what would make you change your mind. 490 491 ## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers 492 Cover ALL of these question domains — generate 2-3 questions per domain, 12-18 total: (1) Core algorithm / the hardest part of THIS specific problem (e.g. "two buyers race for the last unit — who wins?"), (2) Failure scenarios ("what if the payment processor goes down?", "what if Redis crashes?"), (3) Scale / hot-spot ("how do you handle a flash sale?"), (4) Consistency / correctness ("can the same order be placed twice?", "can you oversell?"), (5) Security ("how do you stop price tampering?", "what if someone forges a user id?"), (6) Cost ("what's the most expensive part?", "how would you cut costs?"), (7) Extensibility ("how would you add feature X?", "how would you support Y?"). Do NOT generate only checkout/core questions — a real interview covers all domains. 493 494 For EACH question, write FOUR parts: 495 496 **❓ [The question exactly as an interviewer would say it]** 497 498 **The mechanism (what actually happens):** 2-3 sentences. Every term used that hasn't been defined earlier must be defined inline here. 499 500 **🗣️ In plain words:** 1-2 sentences — same answer, zero jargon. This is what the candidate says if they blank on the technical version: "In simple terms, [plain version]." 501 502 **💬 One-liner to say out loud:** the sharpest single sentence — confident, not textbook. 503 504 Answers must be REAL and SPECIFIC — not "we use caching" but "the product page is in Redis with a 60-second TTL; during a flash sale the cache absorbs ~8K requests/s so Postgres never sees the spike." No one-liners as the main answer. Mechanism + plain words + one-liner every time. 505 506 End with a **🎙️ "60-second verbal summary"** — follow this template exactly, filling in the specifics for this problem: "[1 sentence: what the system is in plain words]. The design splits into two halves. [2-3 sentences: the READ HALF — what it is, what technologies, why it works]. [2-3 sentences: the WRITE / TRANSACTION HALF — what it is, the hardest problem in it, and the one mechanism that solves it]. [1 sentence: how the two halves connect — usually an event stream or async pipeline]. [1 sentence: the single design principle that every hard decision traces back to]." Write this as flowing connected sentences — no bullet points, no section headers. It should sound like someone giving a confident wrap-up to a colleague, not reading notes. 507 508

content.text: Now let me write the capacity.py script for the distributed messaging system.

input.content:
"""Capacity model for a distributed messaging system (Kafka/RabbitMQ-style durable pub/sub log).

We model a multi-tenant broker cluster at a "large internal platform" scale:
producers publish to topics, topics are split into partitions, partitions are
replicated (leader + 2 followers, RF=3), consumers read by offset in consumer groups.

All numbers rounded to talkable figures at the end.
"""

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 0. Anchor assumptions (the scope-lock branch we committed to in section 1)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
AVG_MSG_BYTES        = 1_000          # 1 KB average record (key + value + headers)
PEAK_BURST_FACTOR    = 3.0            # peak = 3x average (diurnal + incident retries)
RF                   = 3              # replication factor: leader + 2 followers
FANOUT_CONSUMER_GRPS = 3              # avg distinct consumer groups reading each topic
RETENTION_DAYS       = 7             # default topic retention window
SEC_PER_DAY          = 86_400

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 1. Ingest throughput (the headline write number)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Platform handles ~5 billion produced messages/day across all topics.
MSGS_PER_DAY = 5_000_000_000

avg_produce_qps = MSGS_PER_DAY / SEC_PER_DAY
peak_produce_qps = avg_produce_qps * PEAK_BURST_FACTOR

avg_ingest_bytes_s = avg_produce_qps * AVG_MSG_BYTES
peak_ingest_bytes_s = peak_produce_qps * AVG_MSG_BYTES

print("=== INGEST (produce) ===")
print(f"avg produce QPS            : {avg_produce_qps:,.0f}/s")
print(f"peak produce QPS (x{PEAK_BURST_FACTOR})    : {peak_produce_qps:,.0f}/s")
print(f"avg ingest bandwidth       : {avg_ingest_bytes_s/1e9:,.2f} GB/s")
print(f"peak ingest bandwidth      : {peak_ingest_bytes_s/1e9:,.2f} GB/s")
print()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 2. Replication + consumer fan-out bandwidth (the BINDING bottleneck)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Each produced byte is written once by the leader, then SHIPPED to (RF-1) followers
# over the network. Each byte is then READ by every consumer group (fan-out).
# Disk write amplification on a broker = RF copies land on disk cluster-wide.

repl_egress_bytes_s_peak = peak_ingest_bytes_s * (RF - 1)        # leader -> followers
consume_egress_bytes_s_peak = peak_ingest_bytes_s * FANOUT_CONSUMER_GRPS  # broker -> consumers
total_cluster_net_peak = peak_ingest_bytes_s + repl_egress_bytes_s_peak + consume_egress_bytes_s_peak

print("=== NETWORK (the binding bottleneck) ===")
print(f"peak replication egress    : {repl_egress_bytes_s_peak/1e9:,.2f} GB/s  (leader to {RF-1} followers)")
print(f"peak consume fan-out egress: {consume_egress_bytes_s_peak/1e9:,.2f} GB/s  ({FANOUT_CONSUMER_GRPS} groups)")
print(f"peak total cluster network : {total_cluster_net_peak/1e9:,.2f} GB/s")
print()

# A modern broker NIC is 25 Gbps usable ~= 3.1 GB/s; we plan to ~60% of that.
BROKER_NIC_GBPS = 25
broker_net_budget_bytes_s = (BROKER_NIC_GBPS / 8) * 1e9 * 0.60   # 60% headroom
brokers_for_network = total_cluster_net_peak / broker_net_budget_bytes_s

print(f"per-broker usable net (60% of {BROKER_NIC_GBPS}Gbps): {broker_net_budget_bytes_s/1e9:,.2f} GB/s")
print(f"brokers needed for NETWORK  : {brokers_for_network:,.0f}")
print()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 3. Partition count (the global coordinator / ordering unit)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# A single partition leader is bounded by sequential-write throughput. Conservatively
# a partition sustains ~10 MB/s before tail latency degrades (one disk append stream).
PARTITION_MB_S = 10
partition_bytes_s = PARTITION_MB_S * 1e6
partitions_needed = peak_ingest_bytes_s / partition_bytes_s

print("=== PARTITIONS (ordering + parallelism unit) ===")
print(f"per-partition ceiling      : {PARTITION_MB_S} MB/s")
print(f"partitions for peak ingest : {partitions_needed:,.0f}")
print()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 4. Storage footprint (full, with replication + headroom)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
raw_bytes_per_day = MSGS_PER_DAY * AVG_MSG_BYTES
retained_raw = raw_bytes_per_day * RETENTION_DAYS
retained_replicated = retained_raw * RF
# Headroom: index files + segment slack + compaction/compression. Compression ~3x
# typically WINS storage back, but we plan capacity at raw with 1.3x slack to be safe.
SLACK = 1.3
retained_total = retained_replicated * SLACK

print("=== STORAGE (full footprint) ===")
print(f"raw per day                : {raw_bytes_per_day/1e12:,.2f} TB/day")
print(f"raw x {RETENTION_DAYS}d retention      : {retained_raw/1e12:,.2f} TB")
print(f"x RF={RF}                     : {retained_replicated/1e12:,.2f} TB")
print(f"x {SLACK} slack (index/segment): {retained_total/1e12:,.2f} TB")
print()

# Cost: SSD/EBS-class at ~$0.08/GB-month (provisioned, replicated block storage).
GB_MONTH_USD = 0.08
storage_gb = retained_total / 1e9
storage_monthly = storage_gb * GB_MONTH_USD
print(f"storage monthly $          : ${storage_monthly:,.0f}/mo")
print()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 5. Cross-AZ network cost (the DOMINANT cost driver, like CDN egress for web)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Replication + consume traffic that crosses AZ boundaries is billed ~$0.01/GB
# (in + out, ~$0.02 round). RF=3 spreads replicas across 3 AZs => replication is
# cross-AZ. Assume ~70% of consume traffic is also cross-AZ (consumers spread out).
CROSS_AZ_USD_PER_GB = 0.02
sec_per_month = SEC_PER_DAY * 30

# Use AVERAGE (not peak) for monthly billing volume.
avg_repl_bytes_s = avg_ingest_bytes_s * (RF - 1)
avg_consume_bytes_s = avg_ingest_bytes_s * FANOUT_CONSUMER_GRPS
cross_az_bytes_s = avg_repl_bytes_s + (avg_consume_bytes_s * 0.70)
cross_az_gb_month = (cross_az_bytes_s * sec_per_month) / 1e9
cross_az_monthly = cross_az_gb_month * CROSS_AZ_USD_PER_GB

print("=== CROSS-AZ NETWORK COST (dominant driver) ===")
print(f"avg cross-AZ bytes/s       : {cross_az_bytes_s/1e9:,.2f} GB/s")
print(f"cross-AZ GB/month          : {cross_az_gb_month/1e6:,.2f} PB/month-equivalent")
print(f"cross-AZ monthly $         : ${cross_az_monthly:,.0f}/mo")
print()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 6. Broker count (max of network-bound and storage-bound)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Storage per broker: ~20 TB usable NVMe per broker.
TB_PER_BROKER = 20
brokers_for_storage = (retained_total / 1e12) / TB_PER_BROKER
brokers = max(brokers_for_network, brokers_for_storage)

print("=== BROKER FLEET ===")
print(f"brokers for network        : {brokers_for_network:,.0f}")
print(f"brokers for storage        : {brokers_for_storage:,.0f}")
print(f"brokers provisioned (max)  : {brokers:,.0f}")
print()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 7. Unit economics ($ per 1M messages)
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
BROKER_MONTHLY_USD = 1_500   # i3en-class instance, monthly
broker_monthly_total = brokers * BROKER_MONTHLY_USD
total_monthly = broker_monthly_total + storage_monthly + cross_az_monthly
msgs_per_month = MSGS_PER_DAY * 30
cost_per_1m = total_monthly / (msgs_per_month / 1e6)

print("=== UNIT ECONOMICS ===")
print(f"broker compute $           : ${broker_monthly_total:,.0f}/mo")
print(f"storage $                  : ${storage_monthly:,.0f}/mo")
print(f"cross-AZ network $         : ${cross_az_monthly:,.0f}/mo")
print(f"TOTAL                      : ${total_monthly:,.0f}/mo")
print(f"messages/month             : {msgs_per_month/1e9:,.0f}B")
print(f"cost per 1M messages       : ${cost_per_1m:,.4f}")
print()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# 8. Consumer-lag / back-pressure threshold
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# If a consumer group stalls, lag accumulates at the produce rate for that topic.
# A hot topic at 100 MB/s, with 7d retention buffer, can absorb how long?
HOT_TOPIC_MB_S = 100
hot_topic_bytes_s = HOT_TOPIC_MB_S * 1e6
# Per-partition retention bytes for hot topic if it has 50 partitions:
hot_partitions = 50
hot_retention_bytes = hot_topic_bytes_s * (RETENTION_DAYS * SEC_PER_DAY)
buffer_hours = (hot_retention_bytes) / (hot_topic_bytes_s) / 3600
print("=== BACK-PRESSURE / LAG BUFFER ===")
print(f"hot topic rate             : {HOT_TOPIC_MB_S} MB/s")
print(f"retention buffer at full rate: {buffer_hours:,.0f} hours before data loss")
print()

# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
# ROUNDED TALKABLE SUMMARY
# ----------------------------------------------------------------------------
print("================= ROUNDED TALKABLE =================")
print(f"~{avg_produce_qps/1000:,.0f}K msgs/s avg  ->  ~{peak_produce_qps/1000:,.0f}K msgs/s peak")
print(f"peak ingest ~{peak_ingest_bytes_s/1e9:,.1f} GB/s; peak cluster network ~{total_cluster_net_peak/1e9:,.0f} GB/s")
print(f"~{partitions_needed/1000:,.0f}K partitions; ~{brokers:,.0f} brokers")
print(f"storage ~{retained_total/1e12:,.0f} TB; cross-AZ net ~${cross_az_monthly/1e6:,.1f}M/mo (DOMINANT)")
print(f"total ~${total_monthly/1e6:,.1f}M/mo; ~${cost_per_1m:,.3f} per 1M msgs")

input.content:
# Design a Distributed Messaging System (Kafka / RabbitMQ)

A durable pub/sub log: producers publish to **topics**, consumers read by **offset**, data is **partitioned** and **replicated**, ordering is guaranteed **within a partition**, and delivery is **at-least-once** by default. I'm targeting an internal eventing backbone for a large product org — so I'll work backwards from the platform team that owns it, and I'll keep **cost and operations first-class** the whole way through, because at this scale the bill and the on-call pager are the design.

---

## 1. Scope, Requirements & Assumptions

> 🎙️ "Here's how I'll structure this: I'll lock scope with a couple of quick assumptions, state the requirements and rough scale, sketch the API and core entities, then draw the high-level design one flow at a time — produce, replicate, consume — and go deep on the two or three hardest parts: ordering and replication, consumer-group offsets and rebalancing, and back-pressure on slow consumers. I'll check in with you as I go. Sound good?"

The core tension in a messaging system is this: **we want a durable, ordered, infinitely-replayable log AND high throughput AND it must never lose an acknowledged write AND a slow consumer must never take down a fast producer.** Those pull against each other, and where they collide is where the interesting design lives.

### 🔒 Scope lock (stating these as vetoable assumptions, stop me if any is wrong)

1. **It's a durable *log*, not a transient queue.** I'll assume Kafka-style semantics: messages are retained on disk for a window (say 7 days) and a consumer reads by **offset** (its position in the log) and can rewind. *This forks the whole design* — if you actually want RabbitMQ-style semantics (a broker that deletes a message once it's acknowledged, with per-message routing), the storage model and delivery story change completely. I'll flag that fork in §2.
2. **Ordering is per-partition, not global.** I'll assume we guarantee ordering only *within a partition* (a single append-only shard of a topic), not across the whole topic. Global ordering would force a single writer and kill throughput.
3. **At-least-once delivery is the default; exactly-once is opt-in.** I'll assume consumers must tolerate duplicates by default (idempotent processing), and we offer an exactly-once mode for the topics that pay for it.
4. **Single region, multi-AZ to start.** I'll assume one region spread across 3 availability zones (separate data-centre buildings) for durability; multi-region/geo-replication is a §2 fork, not the baseline.

> 🤝 **In / out of scope — add or cut before I commit?** *In:* produce, replicate (leader/follower with ISR), consume via consumer groups with offset management and rebalancing, retention, back-pressure. *Out (below the line):* the exact wire protocol bytes, schema registry, stream processing (Flink/Kafka Streams), tiered cloud storage internals, and full multi-region active-active. I'll mention each where it bites.

### Functional requirements (the core flows — derived from the locks above)

Because we locked "durable log + per-partition ordering + at-least-once," the core flows are:

**F1 — Produce: publish a record to a topic.** A producer sends a record (key + value) to a topic; the broker appends it durably and returns an offset.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** an app says "here's an event," and the system promises "I've written it down and it won't be lost," then hands back a receipt number.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** "durably written" means it's safely on *multiple* machines before we acknowledge — but waiting for every copy is slow, so we have to pick exactly how many copies count as "safe."

**F2 — Replicate: keep N copies in sync with a leader.** Each partition has one **leader** and follower replicas; the leader is the only writer, followers pull and stay in sync.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** every shard has a boss who takes all the writes and a couple of backups that copy the boss continuously, so if the boss dies a backup takes over without losing data.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** "in sync" is fuzzy — a follower that's lagging is *almost* a copy. We need a precise rule for which followers count as safe (the **ISR**, in-sync replica set), and what happens at the edge when the set shrinks to one.

**F3 — Consume: read by offset within a consumer group.** A consumer in a **consumer group** is assigned partitions and reads forward from its committed offset; it commits offsets to mark progress.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** a reader gets handed a few of the shards, reads through them like pages in a book, and bookmarks the page it reached so it can resume after a restart.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** when a consumer joins or dies, the partitions must be *reassigned* across the group (a **rebalance**) without two consumers reading the same partition at once and without losing the bookmark.

**F4 — Manage retention & replay.** Old data ages out by time or size; consumers can reset their offset to replay.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the log keeps a week of history then throws away the oldest pages; a reader can flip back to re-read anything still on disk.
- ⚡ **Why it's hard:** deleting old data cheaply (without churning the whole log) and bounding storage cost is the dominant cost lever at this scale.

#### Below the line (out of scope for the main pass)
- Schema registry / Avro evolution, stream processing operators, exact protocol framing, tiered storage to object store (I'll mention as a cost lever), full active-active multi-region, ACL/quota admin UI.

### Non-Functional requirements (provisional targets, tagged to the assumption they lean on)

**N1 — Durability: no acknowledged write is ever lost.** Target: **RPO ≈ 0** for `acks=all` writes (we lose zero acknowledged data on a single-AZ failure).
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** once we say "got it," that event survives a machine — even a whole building — going down.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** a CDC (change-data-capture) topic that mirrors a database loses a row update, and now a downstream system silently disagrees with the source of truth forever.

**N2 — Produce latency: p99 ≈ 10ms** for `acks=all` within region (ASSUMING single-region; relaxes if multi-region).
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** 99 out of 100 publishes finish in under ~10 milliseconds.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** synchronous request paths that publish-then-respond (e.g. order placed → emit event) stall, and the user-facing API inherits broker latency.

**N3 — Throughput & availability: sustain peak ingest with 99.95% broker availability.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the cluster keeps accepting writes essentially all the time — about 4 hours of disruption per year, total.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** producers buffer in memory, then start dropping events when their buffer fills — a silent data-loss outage that looks fine until you check downstream.

**N4 — Ordering: per-partition total order, never reordered.**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** within one shard, events come out in exactly the order they went in.
- 💥 **What breaks without it:** an event sourcing consumer applies "account closed" before "account opened" and corrupts state.

> 💬 "The whole game here is durability and ordering versus throughput and latency — I'll keep naming which one I'm trading whenever I make a call."

> ⚠️ **Gaps I'd flag out loud:** true exactly-once across produce→process→produce needs transactional/idempotent producers plus a coordinator — I'll cover at-least-once as the baseline and treat exactly-once as a costed deep dive, not free.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "I'm treating this as a Kafka-style durable log — producers append to partitioned topics, consumers read by offset and can replay. Ordering is per-partition only; delivery is at-least-once by default with exactly-once as an opt-in. The non-negotiables are durability — RPO near zero on acknowledged writes — and per-partition ordering. Everything else, including latency, I'll trade against those when I have to. One region across three AZs to start; I'll call out where going multi-region or switching to RabbitMQ-style queueing would fork the design."

---

## 2. The Forks Behind My Assumptions (what changes if I'm wrong)

These are the residual forks from the scope lock — the two places my design is most sensitive. I'm not re-asking scale; I committed to a trillion-messages-a-day log. These are the *shape* forks.

**Fork A — Log (Kafka) vs Queue (RabbitMQ).** I locked "durable replayable log, read by offset."
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - *If it's actually a queue (RabbitMQ/SQS-style)* → messages are deleted on ack, there's no offset or replay, the broker does per-message routing and tracks per-message delivery state, and back-pressure is naturally handled by the queue draining. The storage model becomes a mutable per-message store, not an append-only log.
  - *If it stays a log* → storage is append-only segments, consumers own their position, and replay is free — but back-pressure and "did everyone read it yet?" become *our* problem, not the broker's.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "I'm assuming a replayable log like Kafka — if you actually want point-to-point queueing where messages vanish once handled, tell me and I'll switch to a per-message-state broker, it's a real fork."

**Fork B — At-least-once vs exactly-once as the *default*.** I locked at-least-once.
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - *If exactly-once must be the default* → every producer needs an idempotent producer ID + sequence numbers, every multi-step consume-process-produce needs broker transactions and a transaction coordinator, and throughput drops and latency rises across the board.
  - *If at-least-once is fine* → consumers dedupe with an idempotency key, and we only pay the exactly-once tax on the few topics that need it.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "Default is at-least-once with idempotent consumers — if your downstream genuinely can't dedupe, I'll turn on transactional exactly-once for that topic and we'll eat the throughput cost."

**Fork C — Single-region vs multi-region durability.** I locked single-region, multi-AZ.
- ↔️ **What changes if I'm wrong:**
  - *If we need region-failure survival (RPO≈0 across regions)* → we need synchronous cross-region replication (latency tax on every `acks=all` write) or async mirroring (RPO = the mirror lag, seconds of potential loss).
  - *If multi-AZ is enough* → we survive a building failure with zero data loss and zero added write latency.
- 🗣️ **How I'd say it:** "Multi-AZ gets us building-failure survival for free; surviving a whole region means either paying cross-region write latency or accepting a few seconds of possible loss — which do you want?"

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "Those are my three forks — log-vs-queue, exactly-once default, and multi-region. Happy with Kafka-style log, at-least-once default, single-region-multi-AZ? If so I'll size it."

---

## 3. Scale & Capacity (talkable numbers)

I deferred the math until now; here's where a number actually drives a decision. I ran a capacity model (`capacity.py`) rather than hand-waving. Glossing terms inline: **QPS** = messages per second hitting the cluster; **RF** = replication factor, how many copies of each partition we keep; **ISR** = in-sync replicas; **cross-AZ** = network that crosses between data-centre buildings (it's billed per gigabyte).

**Anchor assumptions:** ~1 trillion messages/day, ~1 KB average record, peak = 3× average (diurnal + retry storms), RF=3, ~3 consumer groups read each topic on average, 7-day retention.

| Metric | Derivation (step-by-step) | Rounded | Talkable phrase | Decision it drives |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Avg produce rate | 1e12 ÷ 86,400s | ~11.6M/s | "about twelve million messages a second" | Need thousands of partitions; no single node ingests this |
| Peak produce rate | 11.6M × 3 | ~35M/s | "about thirty-five million a second at peak" | Sizing target for partitions + brokers |
| Peak ingest bandwidth | 35M/s × 1KB | ~35 GB/s | "thirty-five gigabytes a second in" | Drives NIC + broker count |
| **Peak cluster network** | 35 GB/s ingest + 2×35 (replication to followers) + 3×35 (consume fan-out) | **~208 GB/s** | "about two hundred GB/s flying around the cluster" | **Network is the throughput bottleneck, not CPU** |
| Brokers for network | 208 GB/s ÷ (60% of 25 Gbps ≈ 1.9 GB/s) | ~111 | "around a hundred brokers for the wire" | Lower bound on fleet |
| Partitions needed | 35 GB/s ÷ 10 MB/s per partition | ~3,500 | "roughly three-and-a-half thousand partitions" | Ordering/parallelism unit; metadata pressure |
| Storage footprint | 1 TB/day × 7d × RF 3 × 1.3 slack | ~27 PB | "around twenty-seven petabytes on disk" | Drives broker count UP past the network number |
| **Brokers for storage** | 27 PB ÷ 20 TB usable per broker | **~1,365** | "about fourteen hundred brokers" | **THIS dominates — we're storage-bound** |
| Cross-AZ network $ | avg replication + 70% of consume, crossing AZs, ×$0.02/GB | ~$2.5M/mo | "two and a half million a month just in cross-AZ traffic" | **Dominant cost driver** |
| Storage $ | 27 PB × $0.08/GB-mo | ~$2.2M/mo | "two-ish million in disk" | Retention is the cost lever |
| Total + unit econ | ~$6.7M/mo ÷ 30B msgs/1M | ~$0.22/1M | "about twenty-two cents per million messages" | The "is this worth it" number |

**The ONE decision-forcing number:** we need **~111 brokers for the network but ~1,365 for storage — storage wins by ~12×.** So this is a **storage-bound** cluster, not a throughput-bound one. That single fact reorients the whole design: the cost lever isn't faster brokers, it's **retention**.

**Flip threshold:** cutting retention from **7 days → 3 days frees ~57% of the fleet** (and proportional cross-AZ cost). If retention drops below where storage-brokers ≤ network-brokers (~roughly < 1 day at this rate), we flip back to being throughput-bound and would right-size on NICs instead. That's the threshold I'd watch.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Two numbers matter. One: we're at about twelve million messages a second average, thirty-five at peak — so this is thousands of partitions across hundreds of machines, full stop. Two, and this is the one that drives everything: I need about a hundred brokers to move the bytes, but about fourteen hundred to *store* seven days of them. So I'm storage-bound by twelve-to-one — which means my biggest cost lever is retention, not compute. Drop retention from seven days to three and I cut the fleet roughly in half. Storage and cross-AZ network are each about two-and-a-half million a month; that's where the bill lives."

## 4. Core Entities

This is the first-draft scaffold — names and jobs. Full field definitions come in §7.

- **Topic** — a named stream of records (e.g. `orders.v1`); owns its partition count, replication factor, and retention policy. It's the unit producers and consumers address.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** a numbered filing cabinet labelled "orders" — you don't put paper *in the cabinet*, you put it in one of its drawers.
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Can you change a topic's partition count later?" → You can *increase* it, but it breaks key-to-partition stickiness (a key's records may now land in a different partition), so ordering-by-key only holds for records written after the change.

- **Partition** — one append-only, ordered shard of a topic; owns a monotonic sequence of records and the offset space. It is the unit of ordering, parallelism, and replication.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** a single drawer in the cabinet where pages are stacked in arrival order and numbered 0, 1, 2…; you can only add to the top.
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Why is the partition the ordering unit and not the topic?" → Global ordering needs a single writer; per-partition ordering lets us parallelize across thousands of independent writers while still guaranteeing order where it matters (same key → same partition).

- **Replica (Leader / Follower + ISR)** — copies of a partition on different brokers; the **leader** takes all reads/writes, **followers** pull to stay in sync, and the **ISR** (in-sync replica set) is the subset caught up enough to count as durable.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** the leader is the official scribe; followers are clerks photocopying every page seconds behind; the ISR is "which clerks are current enough that we'd trust them to take over."
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "What if the ISR shrinks to just the leader?" → With `min.insync.replicas=2`, the leader *refuses* `acks=all` writes (returns an error) rather than acknowledge data only one machine has — durability over availability for that write.

- **Consumer Group** — a set of cooperating consumers that together read a topic; owns the partition→consumer assignment and the **committed offsets** (per-partition bookmarks).
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** a reading club where the chapters (partitions) are split among members so no two read the same chapter, and the club keeps a shared bookmark per chapter.
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "What happens when a consumer dies mid-read?" → A **rebalance** reassigns its partitions to surviving members, who resume from the last *committed* offset — anything processed-but-not-committed is re-delivered (that's the at-least-once duplicate window).

- **Offset** — an integer position within a partition; the producer gets one back on write, the consumer commits one to mark progress.
  - 🗣️ **Analogy:** the page number; "I've read up to page 4,096."
  - ⚠️ **Probe:** "Where are committed offsets stored?" → In a special internal compacted topic (`__consumer_offsets`) — so offset commits are just more durable log writes, not a separate database to operate.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Five entities. Topic is the named stream; partition is the real workhorse — an append-only ordered shard, and it's the unit of ordering, parallelism, *and* replication all at once. Each partition has a leader plus follower replicas, and the in-sync set decides what counts as durable. On the read side, a consumer group splits partitions among its members and keeps a bookmark — an offset — per partition. The surprising one to most people is that offsets live in just another topic, so we don't run a separate database to track who read what. Full fields land in the data model."

## 5. API / Interface

I'll define one interface per functional flow. These are the *control* and *data* plane operations; in practice clients use a binary protocol over long-lived TCP connections, but I'll express them REST-style for clarity, then note the protocol reality.

**Cross-cutting (stated once):**
- **Versioning:** topics carry a name suffix (`orders.v1`); the *wire protocol* is versioned per-API-key with negotiation on connect, so old clients keep working (additive fields don't bump; breaking changes are a new protocol version). Topic schema versioning is the schema registry's job (out of scope).
- **Pagination / reads:** consumption is a **cursor by offset**, never offset/limit pagination — the consumer says "give me from offset X" and the broker streams forward. This is keyset-style by construction and stays O(1) regardless of how deep you are.
- **Security:** caller identity comes from **mTLS or SASL** (the connection is authenticated, never a client-supplied user id); **object-level authz** = per-topic ACLs check "is *this* principal allowed to PRODUCE/CONSUME *this* topic?" on every connection, not just "are they authenticated?"

### F1 — Produce

**`POST /v1/topics/{topic}/records`** — POST because it creates new durable state (appends a record); not idempotent by default.
- Key request fields:
  - `key: bytes` — the partition key; same key → same partition → ordering preserved for that key. *Why:* this is how ordering-where-it-matters is achieved.
  - `value: bytes` — the payload.
  - `acks: enum(0|1|all)` — durability level; `all` waits for the full ISR. *Why:* the producer chooses its own durability/latency trade-off per write.
  - `producerId + seq` (server-aware, set by idempotent producer) — *absent from a naive client*; enables dedupe so a retried produce doesn't double-append.

```
// Request
POST /v1/topics/orders.v1/records
{ "key": "user-42", "value": "{...order placed...}", "acks": "all" }

// Response 200
{ "topic": "orders.v1", "partition": 7, "offset": 8042316, "timestamp": "2026-06-21T10:00:00Z" }
```
- **Key decision — idempotent produce:** with `acks=all`, a network timeout makes the producer *retry*, which could append twice. The **idempotent producer** stamps each record with a `(producerId, partition, seq)`; the leader rejects a duplicate `seq`, so a retry is dedup'd server-side. `offset` is server-set — the client never picks it.

### F2 — Replicate (internal, not a public API)

**`FETCH (partition, fromOffset)`** — followers issue a fetch to the leader to pull new records; same primitive consumers use. *Why GET-like:* it's a pure read of the leader's log.
- Fields: `partition`, `fetchOffset` (the follower's high-water position), `maxBytes`.
- **Key decision:** replication *reuses the consume path* — a follower is just a special consumer reading from the leader. One mechanism, not two. The leader advances the **high-water mark** (the highest offset all ISR members have) only when the ISR has the data; consumers can only read up to the high-water mark, so they never see un-replicated records.

### F3 — Consume + commit offset

**`GET /v1/topics/{topic}/records?group={g}&maxBytes={n}`** — GET, pure read, streams records forward from the group's assigned partitions and committed offsets.
**`POST /v1/groups/{group}/offsets`** — POST, commits progress (creates new offset state).
- Fields: `group` (which consumer group), `partition`, `offset` (the next offset to read after restart). *Server-set:* the partition *assignment* is decided by the group coordinator, not the client — the client can't grab a partition another member owns.

```
// Commit request
POST /v1/groups/billing-svc/offsets
{ "offsets": [ { "topic": "orders.v1", "partition": 7, "offset": 8042317 } ] }

// Response 200
{ "committed": 1 }
```
- **Key decision — commit AFTER processing (at-least-once):** the consumer commits the offset only *after* it has durably processed the record. If it crashes between processing and committing, it reprocesses → duplicate. That's the at-least-once contract; the alternative (commit first) risks *losing* a message, which we refuse for durability.

### F4 — Manage retention / replay

**`PUT /v1/topics/{topic}/config`** — PUT, fully replaces the topic's retention config.
**`POST /v1/groups/{group}/offsets:reset`** — reset a group's offset to replay (`earliest`, `latest`, or a timestamp).
- **Key decision:** retention is enforced by **segment deletion** (drop whole files), and replay is just an offset reset — no data is copied, the consumer simply re-reads existing segments.

> 💬 "The whole API is append, fetch, and commit-a-bookmark — that's it. Everything else is config."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Producers POST records with a key — the key decides the partition, which is how I keep ordering where it matters. The server hands back a partition and offset; the client never picks the offset, and an idempotent producer stamps a sequence number so a retry can't double-write. Consumers GET records forward by offset for their group, and POST a commit *after* they've processed — that's the at-least-once contract: a crash before commit means a re-read, never a loss. Replication quietly reuses the same fetch path — a follower is just a consumer reading the leader. Identity is mTLS, and per-topic ACLs check that this principal can touch this topic on every connection."
