## 6. High-Level Design (built one functional requirement at a time)

I'll define the color language once and reuse it: blue = clients/producers/consumers, green = our broker services, amber = the log/async storage, grey = metadata + offset stores, purple = external.

### 6.1 — F1 Produce: publish a record to a topic

The producer must find the *leader* of the right partition, send the record, and only get an ack once the data is durable per its chosen `acks`.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Partitioner (client-side) | Logic in the producer that hashes the key to pick a partition | Keeps partition selection off the broker hot path; same key always lands together | Not server-side routing: adds a network hop and a broker bottleneck per write | Client must know partition count; a partition increase breaks key stickiness |
| Broker (partition leader) | A server process that owns the append-only log for its partitions | Only the leader writes, giving a single total order per partition for free | Not multi-writer per partition: would need consensus per record, killing throughput | Leader is a per-partition single point; failover needed (see 6.2) |
| Append-only segment log | The on-disk file the leader appends records to, split into segments | Sequential disk append is the fastest durable write pattern there is | Not a B-tree/row store: random writes are 10-100x slower and we never update in place | No in-place edits; deletes are whole-segment only |
| Metadata service | The cluster brain that tracks which broker leads which partition | Producers cache it and route directly; one source of truth for leadership | Not gossip-only: slower to converge, ambiguous leadership during churn | It's a global coordinator — must be HA (see §8) |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Partition leader** — the one broker that accepts writes for a partition. Here it gives us a single total order per partition without any cross-machine coordination per record.
> **Append-only segment log** — a file you only ever add to the end of, rolled into fixed-size segments. Here it makes writes a sequential disk append (very fast) and makes deletion a cheap whole-file drop.
> **Metadata service** — the cluster's directory of "which broker leads which partition." Here producers cache it so they send directly to the right leader with no extra hop.
> **acks** — the durability dial on a write: `1` = leader only, `all` = the whole in-sync set. Here it lets each producer trade latency for safety.

**Diagram — Produce path (acks=all)**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Producer app]
    P -->|"1 ask for leader"| Meta[(Metadata service)]
    P -->|"2 send record acks all"| Lead[Broker partition leader]
    subgraph Replication["Partition replica set"]
        Lead -->|"3 append to log"| Log[[Append only segment log]]
        Lead -->|"4 followers fetch"| F1b[Broker follower one]
        Lead -->|"4 followers fetch"| F2b[Broker follower two]
    end
    F1b -->|"5 ack to leader"| Lead
    F2b -->|"5 ack to leader"| Lead
    Lead -->|"6 ack with offset"| P
```
↳ reuses existing: Metadata service (introduced here), broker leader/follower (6.2 extends).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Producer asks for the leader** — it queries (or reads from cache) the metadata service for "who leads partition 7 of `orders.v1`." *Why metadata:* it's the single directory of leadership, so the producer routes directly with no broker-side proxy hop. *State change:* none yet. *What could go wrong:* metadata is stale after a failover → producer gets `NotLeader`, refreshes metadata, retries.
2. **Producer sends the record to the leader** with `acks=all`. *Why the leader:* only the leader writes, so this is where the single total order is assigned. *State change:* none committed yet. *Failure:* leader down mid-send → producer times out, refreshes metadata, retries (idempotent producer prevents a double-append).
3. **Leader appends to its log** and assigns the next offset. *State change:* record is on the leader's disk with offset 8042316, but NOT yet acknowledged. *Failure:* disk full → leader rejects, producer back-pressured.
4–5. **Followers fetch and ack** — the two ISR followers pull the record and confirm. *Why:* `acks=all` means we don't acknowledge until the ISR has it. *State change:* high-water mark advances to include 8042316. *Failure:* a follower lags → it drops out of the ISR; if ISR < `min.insync.replicas`, the write is refused (durability over availability).
6. **Leader acks the producer** with the assigned offset. *State change:* the write is now durable and visible to consumers (it's ≤ high-water mark). *Failure:* ack lost on the wire → producer retries → idempotent dedupe.

> 💬 "The producer talks straight to the partition leader — and the acknowledgment doesn't come back until enough copies have it. That one rule is my entire durability guarantee."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "A producer hashes the key to pick a partition, looks up that partition's leader, and sends the record. The leader appends it to an append-only log — sequential disk writes, the fastest durable pattern — and assigns an offset. With acks=all, it waits until the in-sync followers have copied the record before it acknowledges. That's the durability guarantee in one sentence: I don't say 'got it' until more than one machine has it. The trade-off is latency — waiting for replicas costs a few milliseconds — but for a system whose whole job is to not lose data, that's the right call."

### 6.2 — F2 Replicate: leader/follower with ISR and failover

This slice adds the *resilience* — what keeps a partition alive when its leader dies, without losing acknowledged data.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ISR (in-sync replica set) | The set of followers caught up to the leader within a lag bound | Durability rule: acked data lives on every ISR member, so any can take over losslessly | Not "all replicas must ack": one slow disk would stall every write | A lagging follower silently drops out, shrinking durability margin |
| Controller (leader elector) | The cluster role that detects a dead broker and elects a new partition leader from the ISR | Centralized election is fast and unambiguous; picks only from ISR so no data loss | Not per-partition Paxos: thousands of partitions × consensus = unmanageable | The controller is a global coordinator — needs its own HA (Raft quorum) |
| High-water mark | The highest offset all ISR members have; the read ceiling for consumers | Consumers never see un-replicated data, so a failover can't "un-send" a record they read | Not "read the leader's latest": a consumer could see data that vanishes on failover | Consumers read slightly behind the leader's tail |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **ISR (in-sync replica set)** — the followers that are caught up enough to be trustworthy backups. Here it defines what "durable" means: acked data is on every ISR member.
> **Controller** — the elected cluster coordinator that re-assigns leadership when a broker dies. Here it picks the new leader only from the ISR, so failover never loses acknowledged data.
> **High-water mark** — the offset boundary that's safely replicated. Here it's the furthest a consumer is allowed to read, so a failover can never retract a record a consumer already saw.

**Diagram — Leader failure and election**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Ctrl[Controller raft quorum]
    Lead[Broker old leader]
    Ctrl -->|"1 detect leader dead"| Lead
    Ctrl -->|"2 pick new leader from ISR"| NewLead[Broker former follower now leader]
    subgraph Survivors["Surviving replica set"]
        NewLead -->|"3 serve writes"| Log[[Append only segment log]]
        NewLead -->|"4 followers refetch"| F2b[Broker other follower]
    end
    NewLead -->|"5 update metadata"| Meta[(Metadata service)]
    P[Producer app] -->|"6 refresh and resend"| NewLead
```
↳ reuses existing: Producer, Metadata service, segment log (from 6.1).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Controller detects the dead leader** via missed heartbeats. *Why controller:* one authority decides leadership, avoiding split-brain. *State:* the partition is briefly leaderless (writes pause). *Failure:* controller itself dies → its Raft quorum (a small odd-numbered group that votes) elects a new controller in seconds.
2. **Controller elects a new leader from the ISR** — never from an out-of-sync replica. *Why ISR-only:* an out-of-sync replica is missing acked data; promoting it would lose it. *State:* a former follower is now leader. *Failure:* ISR is empty (all replicas lagged) → either accept unclean election (data loss, configurable) or stay unavailable (we choose unavailable for money topics).
3–4. **New leader serves writes; remaining follower re-fetches** to re-sync. *State:* the partition is writable again, ISR rebuilds. *Failure:* the new leader also dies → repeat election.
5. **Metadata updated** so producers/consumers learn the new leader. *State:* leadership directory is current.
6. **Producer refreshes and resends** any in-flight record. *State:* idempotent producer ensures no double-append. *Failure:* none new — this is the recovery.

> 💬 "Failover only ever promotes a replica that already has every acknowledged byte — that's why a crash can't lose data."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Each partition has a leader and a couple of followers, and the in-sync set is the followers that are caught up. When the leader dies, a controller — backed by its own small Raft voting group so it's never a single point — detects it and promotes a follower, but *only* one from the in-sync set, so the new leader already has every acknowledged byte. Consumers only ever read up to the high-water mark, the offset everyone has, so a failover can't retract something they already saw. The trade-off is that if the in-sync set shrinks to nothing, I'd rather refuse writes than promote a stale replica and lose data."

### 6.3 — F3 Consume: consumer groups, offsets, and rebalancing

This slice adds the read side and the hardest coordination problem: dividing partitions among a changing set of consumers without overlap or loss.

**Architecture decisions table**

| Component | What it is (plain English) | Why THIS choice | What we DIDN'T pick & why | Trade-off accepted |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Group coordinator | A broker role that tracks group membership and owns the partition→consumer assignment | One authority per group prevents two consumers owning the same partition | Not client-side gossip: races and split assignments under churn | Coordinator failure triggers a rebalance |
| Committed offsets topic | An internal compacted topic storing each group's per-partition bookmark | Offsets are just durable log writes — no separate DB to operate | Not an external SQL/KV store: another system to scale, replicate, and page on | Compaction lag means offset reads are eventually consistent (fine — they're a floor) |
| Rebalance protocol | The handshake that reassigns partitions when membership changes | Cooperative/incremental version moves only the partitions that must move | Not stop-the-world eager rebalance: pauses the whole group on every change | Cooperative rebalance is more protocol rounds (more complex) |

> 🗣️ **Key terms for this slice:**
> **Group coordinator** — the broker that manages one consumer group's membership and assignments. Here it guarantees no two consumers read the same partition at once.
> **Committed offsets topic** — an internal log (`__consumer_offsets`) that stores bookmarks. Here it means offsets are durable for free, with no extra database.
> **Rebalance** — the reassignment of partitions when a consumer joins or leaves. Here the cooperative variant moves only what must move, so most consumers keep reading.
> **Compacted topic** — a log that keeps only the latest value per key. Here it bounds the offsets topic's size to "one entry per group-partition."

**Diagram — Consume + rebalance**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Consumer one in group]
    C2[Consumer two in group]
    Coord[Group coordinator]
    C1 -->|"1 join group"| Coord
    C2 -->|"1 join group"| Coord
    Coord -->|"2 assign partitions"| C1
    Coord -->|"2 assign partitions"| C2
    C1 -->|"3 fetch records"| Lead[Broker partition leader]
    C1 -->|"4 commit offset"| OffLog[(Committed offsets topic)]
    C3[Consumer three joins] -->|"5 trigger rebalance"| Coord
    Coord -->|"6 reassign subset"| C1
```
↳ reuses existing: partition leader (6.1).

**Numbered step narration**
1. **Consumers join the group** by registering with the coordinator. *Why:* the coordinator needs the live membership to assign partitions. *State:* group membership = {C1, C2}. *Failure:* a consumer's heartbeat stops → coordinator evicts it and rebalances.
2. **Coordinator assigns partitions** (e.g. C1 ← partitions 0-3, C2 ← 4-7). *Why coordinator:* single authority = no overlap. *State:* assignment recorded. *Failure:* assignment message lost → consumer re-joins.
3. **Consumer fetches records** from each assigned partition's leader, forward from its committed offset. *State:* consumer position advances in memory. *Failure:* leader failover mid-fetch → consumer refreshes metadata, continues from committed offset.
4. **Consumer commits the offset** *after* processing, to the offsets topic. *State:* durable bookmark advances. *Failure:* crash before commit → on restart, re-reads from last commit → duplicate (at-least-once).
5–6. **A new consumer joins, triggering a rebalance** — the coordinator reassigns a *subset* of partitions to balance load (cooperative: C1 keeps 0-2, gives 3 to C3). *State:* new assignment; only the moved partition pauses briefly. *Failure:* a slow consumer doesn't ack the rebalance → coordinator times it out and proceeds.

> 💬 "The coordinator is the referee — it guarantees no two consumers ever own the same partition, and the bookmark is just another durable log write."

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Consumers in a group register with a coordinator, which splits the partitions among them so no two read the same one. Each consumer reads forward and commits its offset — its bookmark — *after* processing, and that commit is just a write to an internal compacted log, so I don't run a separate offsets database. When a consumer joins or dies, the coordinator rebalances; the cooperative version only moves the partitions that have to move, so the rest of the group keeps reading. The trade-off I accept is the duplicate window: if a consumer crashes after processing but before committing, it'll reprocess on restart — which is exactly why downstream consumers must be idempotent."

### Final (high-level) — the reconciled union

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Prod[Producer app]
    Cons[Consumer in group]
    Meta[(Metadata service)]
    Ctrl[Controller raft quorum]
    Coord[Group coordinator]
    OffLog[(Committed offsets topic)]
    Prod -->|"produce acks all"| Lead
    Prod -.->|"lookup leader"| Meta
    subgraph WritePath["Write and replication path"]
        Lead[Broker partition leader]
        Lead -->|"append"| Log[[Append only segment log]]
        Lead -->|"replicate"| Foll[Broker followers ISR]
    end
    subgraph ReadPath["Read path"]
        Lead -->|"fetch records"| Cons
        Cons -->|"commit offset"| OffLog
        Cons -.->|"join and assign"| Coord
    end
    Ctrl -.->|"elect leaders"| Lead
    Ctrl -.->|"track membership"| Coord
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Write path: a producer finds the leader via metadata, appends with acks=all, and the leader replicates to its in-sync followers before acknowledging. Resilience: a controller, on its own Raft quorum, elects a new leader from the in-sync set if one dies, so we never lose acknowledged data. Read path: consumers join a group, the coordinator hands each one a slice of partitions, they fetch forward and commit bookmarks to an internal offsets topic. The one event stream — the partition log — is shared by everyone; producers append, followers replicate, consumers replay."

> 🤝 **Checkpoint:** "That's the skeleton end to end — produce, replicate, consume, rebalance. Want me deeper on the durability/ISR mechanics, the rebalance and offset story, or back-pressure on slow consumers? I have all three ready."

## 7. Data Model & Storage

### PART A — Entity table

| Entity | Key fields (PK / shard key annotated) | Chosen store | Shard / partition key | Consistency guarantee |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Topic config | `topic_name` **(PK)**, partition_count, RF, retention_ms, cleanup_policy | Metadata store (Raft KV) | replicated to all | Strong (linearizable) |
| Partition log | `(topic, partition)` **(shard key)**, base_offset, records[] | Append-only segment files on broker NVMe | `(topic, partition)` → broker | Per-partition total order, RF=3 |
| Replica state | `(topic, partition, broker)` **(PK)**, log_end_offset, is_in_isr | Metadata store | `(topic, partition)` | Strong (controller-owned) |
| Consumer group | `group_id` **(PK)**, members[], assignment | Group coordinator (broker) | `group_id` → coordinator broker | Strong within coordinator |
| Committed offset | `(group, topic, partition)` **(PK)**, offset | `__consumer_offsets` compacted topic | hash(group) → partition | Read-your-commit (compacted) |

### PART B — Storage decision cards

**🗄️ Append-only segment log (on broker NVMe) — used for: the partition data itself**
1. **What it is:** a set of files on local disk that the broker only ever *appends* to, rolled into fixed-size segments named by their starting offset.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** the entire workload is "append at the tail, read sequentially forward, delete the oldest in bulk." Sequential append + the OS page cache makes both writes and reads hit memory/sequential-disk speeds, and zero-copy `sendfile` ships bytes from page cache straight to the network with no copy through user space.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **A relational DB (Postgres):** every record an INSERT with index maintenance — random I/O, MVCC bloat, and we'd hit a write wall orders of magnitude below 35 GB/s.
   - **A document/KV store (Cassandra/Dynamo):** great for point lookups, but we never look up by key — we stream by offset; their LSM compaction would fight our retention model and cost more.
   - **An object store (S3) as primary:** per-object latency and request cost make it wrong for the hot tail, though it's perfect for a *cold tier* (see lifecycle).
4. **Trade-off we accept:** no in-place updates and no secondary indexes — you can only read by offset, and deletion is whole-segment only.
5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** "The log is just append-only files on local NVMe. The workload is append-and-scan, never random lookup, so sequential I/O plus the OS page cache and zero-copy networking is the fastest thing there is. A database would die under random-write amplification at this rate. The cost is you can only read by offset and only delete whole segments — but that's exactly what a log needs."

**🗄️ Metadata store (Raft-replicated KV) — used for: topic config, leadership, ISR state**
1. **What it is:** a small, strongly-consistent key-value store replicated by Raft (a voting protocol where a majority must agree before a change commits).
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** cluster metadata (who leads what, who's in the ISR) must be *linearizable* — every broker must agree on the single current leader, or two brokers think they're leader and we corrupt the log. The data is tiny (KBs per partition) but the consistency requirement is absolute.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **ZooKeeper (external):** the classic choice, but it's a second system to operate, scale, and page on; the modern move (KRaft) folds it into the brokers.
   - **Gossip-based eventual consistency:** ambiguous leadership during churn → split-brain → data loss. Unacceptable for leadership.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** Raft writes need a majority round-trip, so metadata changes (leader election) take tens of milliseconds — fine, since they're rare.
5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** "Leadership and in-sync-set state live in a small Raft-replicated store, because everyone *must* agree on who the one leader is — if two brokers both think they lead a partition, I've corrupted the log. It's tiny data but it has to be linearizable. I fold it into the brokers rather than running a separate ZooKeeper to operate."

**🗄️ `__consumer_offsets` (internal compacted topic) — used for: committed offsets**
1. **What it is:** an internal Kafka topic that stores `(group, topic, partition) → offset`, **compacted** so it keeps only the latest offset per key.
2. **Why we picked it — the access pattern:** offset commits are high-frequency writes that only ever need the *latest* value per key. A compacted log gives durability and bounded size for free, reusing the exact storage engine we already operate.
3. **What we considered instead:**
   - **External Redis/Postgres:** another stateful system to scale to millions of commits/s and replicate — pure operational drag.
   - **In-memory only:** loses all bookmarks on coordinator restart → mass reprocessing.
4. **Trade-off we accept:** compaction runs asynchronously, so the topic is briefly larger than its compacted floor; offset reads are a *floor* (you never read an offset higher than committed).
5. **🗣️ How to say it out loud:** "Offsets are just stored in another topic — a compacted one, so it keeps only the latest bookmark per group-partition. That means I get durability and reuse the same engine I already run, instead of bolting on a database that has to survive millions of commits a second."

### PART C — Per-operation consistency

| Operation | Store | Consistency level | Why that level is right |
|---|---|---|---|
| Produce `acks=all` | Partition log + ISR | Strong (RF-quorum durable) | Money/CDC topics cannot lose acked data |
| Produce `acks=1` | Partition log (leader) | Leader-only durable | Throughput topics (logs/metrics) tolerate rare loss |
| Leader election | Metadata store | Linearizable | Two leaders = log corruption |
| Consume read | Partition log | Read up to high-water mark | Never expose un-replicated data |
| Offset commit | `__consumer_offsets` | Read-your-commit | A consumer must resume from at least where it committed |
| Read-your-own-write (consumer) | Log | Same offset visible cluster-wide once ≤ high-water mark | After a produce ack, the offset is replicated, so any consumer on any replica sees it |

**Read-your-own-writes trace:** a producer gets ack at offset 8042316 with `acks=all` → that offset is ≤ the high-water mark (the ISR has it) → therefore *any* consumer fetching that partition, from the leader or a follower, can read it. There's no stale-replica hole because consumers are capped at the high-water mark, which by definition is replicated.

### PART D — Data lifecycle (the largest table: the partition log)

At ~1 TB/day raw × RF 3, the partition log is ~27 PB at 7-day retention — that's the ~$2.2M/mo storage line and the reason we're storage-bound. **Reclamation is segment deletion, not row delete:** when a segment's newest record ages past `retention_ms`, the broker drops the *entire segment file* (O(1), instant) — never a per-record DELETE, which would churn indexes and never reclaim cleanly. **Cold tier:** segments older than the hot window (e.g. > 1 day) are offloaded to an object store (S3-class, ~$0.02/GB-mo, ~4× cheaper than NVMe) and read on demand for rare replays — this is the single biggest cost optimization, cutting hot storage roughly in half. **Compacted topics** (offsets, config) reclaim by keeping only the latest value per key instead of by time.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The data is the log — append-only segment files on local NVMe, because the workload is append-and-scan and sequential I/O with the OS page cache and zero-copy networking beats any database here. Cluster metadata — who leads what — lives in a small Raft store because everyone must agree on the one leader; I fold it into the brokers instead of running ZooKeeper. Offsets are stored in another compacted topic, so I reuse the same engine instead of operating a separate database. The big table is the log itself at twenty-seven petabytes; I reclaim it by dropping whole segment files when they age out, and I tier anything older than a day to object storage — that's my biggest cost win. I considered S3 as the *primary* store and rejected it: per-request latency is wrong for the hot tail, but it's perfect as the cold tier."

## 8. Deep Dives — Bad → Good → Great

> 🆘 **If you get stuck:** go back to the two invariants — *acked data is never lost* and *per-partition order is never violated* — and reason forward from there.

### How do we guarantee durability without killing throughput? (the ISR mechanism)

**#### Bad: acks=1, leader-only**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** the leader writes the record to its own disk and immediately says "got it," without waiting for any backup.
- **Approach:** producer sends, leader appends, leader acks. Fast — one disk write, no replica round-trip.
- **Why people try this:** lowest possible latency and highest throughput; it *feels* durable because it's on disk.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** the leader dies after acking but before any follower copied the record → that acknowledged write is **gone**. At ~35M writes/s, even a rare leader crash drops thousands of acked records — for a CDC topic that's permanent silent divergence from the source DB.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** any topic where losing an acknowledged write is unacceptable (money, CDC) can't use `acks=1`.

**#### Good: acks=all to ALL replicas**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** `acks=1` acked data only one machine had; this waits for *every* replica before acking, so a single death can't lose it.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** don't say "got it" until all the backups have copied it too.
- **Approach:** leader appends, waits for *all* N replicas to confirm, then acks.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** one slow follower (a GC pause, a bad disk) stalls *every* write to that partition — tail latency spikes and throughput collapses to the speed of the slowest replica. You've coupled availability to your unluckiest machine.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need durability *without* one slow node holding everyone hostage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Leader appends record]
    L -->|"wait for ALL"| R1[Follower one]
    L -->|"wait for ALL"| R2[Follower two slow GC pause]
    R1 -->|"ack fast"| L
    R2 -->|"ack late blocks everyone"| L
    L -->|"ack only after slowest"| P[Producer waits]
```

**#### Great: ISR + min.insync.replicas**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** waiting for *all* replicas let one slow node stall the partition; the ISR lets a lagging replica drop out so it no longer blocks writes, while still requiring a quorum to be durable.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** keep a "currently caught-up" club; you only need the club members to confirm, and a slow member is quietly kicked out of the club so it can't slow you down — but if the club gets too small, you stop accepting writes rather than risk losing data.
- **Approach:** the leader maintains the **ISR** (followers within a lag bound). `acks=all` means "all *ISR* members," not all replicas. A follower that falls behind is removed from the ISR (stops blocking). `min.insync.replicas=2` means the leader refuses writes if the ISR drops below 2 — so an acked write always lives on ≥2 machines. On leader death, the controller elects only from the ISR → zero acked-data loss.
- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** RF=3, `min.insync=2`. We tolerate **1** replica failure with zero loss *and* zero write-stall (ISR still has 2). A 2nd simultaneous failure → ISR=1 < 2 → writes refused (unavailable, but no loss). So: 3 copies, 2 must ack, survive 1 failure transparently.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| 1 follower slow | drops out of ISR; writes continue on remaining ISR; no loss |
| 1 follower dies | ISR shrinks to 2; writes continue; failed replica re-syncs on return |
| Leader dies | controller promotes an ISR follower (has all acked data); no loss |
| ISR shrinks to 1 | leader refuses `acks=all` writes (min.insync=2); availability sacrificed, no loss |
| Network partition isolates leader | controller elects new leader on majority side; old leader steps down (can't reach ISR) |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L[Leader maintains ISR]
    L -->|"in ISR"| R1[Follower one caught up]
    L -.->|"lagging removed from ISR"| R3[Follower three slow]
    R1 -->|"ack"| L
    L -->|"ack when ISR count meets min insync"| P[Producer]
    L -->|"if ISR below 2 refuse write"| Reject[Write rejected error]
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The in-sync replica set is how I get durability without a slow node holding everyone hostage. The leader tracks which followers are caught up; an acknowledgment only needs the in-sync members, and a follower that lags gets quietly dropped from the set so it can't stall writes. The safety valve is min-insync-replicas — with three copies and a minimum of two, I survive any single failure with zero loss and zero stall, and if the set ever shrinks below two I refuse the write rather than acknowledge data only one machine has. Durability over availability for that write, deliberately."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just acks=all to all replicas?":** "Because one GC pause on one follower would stall every write to that partition. The ISR decouples durability from the slowest machine — I still need a quorum, but a lagging node leaves the quorum instead of blocking it."

### How do we rebalance consumers without losing or double-reading? (offset + rebalance)

**#### Bad: static assignment, commit-then-process**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** hard-code which consumer reads which partition, and bookmark the page *before* you've actually read it.
- **Approach:** fixed partition→consumer map; consumer commits offset, then processes.
- **Why people try this:** simple, no coordinator, and committing first "feels" safe.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** (a) a dead consumer's partitions go *unread* forever — no failover. (b) Commit-then-process *loses* messages: commit offset 100, crash before processing 100 → on restart you resume at 101, and 100 is silently skipped. That's **at-most-once** — data loss.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need automatic reassignment on failure AND a commit order that doesn't drop messages.

**#### Good: group coordinator + eager rebalance + commit-after-process**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** static assignment had no failover and commit-first lost data; a coordinator adds automatic reassignment and committing *after* processing flips us to at-least-once (duplicates, never loss).
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** a referee reassigns chapters when readers come and go, and you only bookmark a page *after* you've actually read it.
- **Approach:** the **group coordinator** tracks membership via heartbeats; on any change it triggers a **stop-the-world rebalance** — every consumer drops its partitions, the coordinator recomputes the full assignment, everyone resumes from committed offsets. Consumers commit *after* processing.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** the stop-the-world rebalance pauses the *entire group* on every join/leave — in an autoscaling fleet of hundreds of consumers, scaling events cause repeated full pauses ("rebalance storms"), and a slow rejoin stalls everyone. Consumer lag spikes on every deploy.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need rebalances that don't pause the whole group.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Coord[Group coordinator]
    Coord -->|"member change"| Stop[All consumers stop and revoke]
    Stop -->|"recompute full assignment"| Assign[Coordinator reassigns everything]
    Assign -->|"resume from committed offsets"| Run[All consumers resume]
    Run -.->|"any join or leave repeats"| Coord
```

**#### Great: cooperative incremental rebalance + idempotent consumers**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** eager rebalance stopped the whole group; cooperative rebalance moves only the partitions that must change owner, so most consumers never pause.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** when one reader joins, only hand over the few chapters that need a new owner — everyone else keeps reading without interruption.
- **Approach:** **cooperative (incremental) rebalance** runs in two phases — the coordinator computes the *diff*, only the consumers losing a partition revoke it, then those partitions are assigned to the new owner. Consumers keep their unaffected partitions throughout. Combined with **commit-after-process** (at-least-once) and **idempotent consumers** (dedupe by a natural key, e.g. `order_id`, in a dedupe store with a TTL), the unavoidable duplicate window on crash becomes harmless.
- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** eager rebalance on a 200-consumer fleet pauses all 200 on every scale event; cooperative pauses only the ~1-2 consumers whose partitions actually move — a ~100× reduction in disrupted consumers per event, turning multi-second group-wide stalls into sub-second localized ones.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| Consumer dies | coordinator times out its heartbeat, reassigns only its partitions to others |
| Consumer crashes after process, before commit | on restart, re-reads from last commit → duplicate → idempotent consumer dedupes |
| Coordinator dies | a new coordinator is elected (it's a partition leader of the offsets topic); group re-discovers it |
| Slow consumer | coordinator evicts it after max-poll timeout; its partitions move; it rejoins later |
| Duplicate delivery | dedupe store keyed by natural key rejects the second apply; effective exactly-once *processing* |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Coord[Group coordinator]
    NewC[New consumer joins]
    NewC -->|"1 join"| Coord
    Coord -->|"2 revoke only moved partition"| C1[Consumer one keeps rest]
    Coord -->|"3 assign moved partition"| NewC
    C1 -->|"4 unaffected partitions keep reading"| Lead[Partition leader]
    NewC -->|"5 process then commit"| OffLog[(Committed offsets topic)]
    NewC -.->|"6 dedupe by natural key"| Dedupe[(Dedupe store TTL)]
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The naive version statically assigns partitions and bookmarks before reading — that has no failover and actually loses data. So I use a coordinator that reassigns on failure, and I commit *after* processing, which makes it at-least-once: a crash before commit means a re-read, never a loss. The upgrade is cooperative rebalancing — when a consumer joins, I only move the one or two partitions that have to change hands, so the other hundred-and-ninety-eight consumers never pause. The leftover duplicate window I close at the consumer with idempotent processing — dedupe by a natural key — so reprocessing a message is a no-op. That's effectively exactly-once *processing* on top of at-least-once *delivery*."

> 🧠 **If they ask "why not just do exactly-once delivery?":** "True exactly-once needs transactional producers and a transaction coordinator across produce-process-produce — real throughput and latency cost. For most topics, at-least-once delivery plus an idempotent consumer is cheaper and gives the same business outcome."

### How do we stop a slow consumer from taking down a fast producer? (back-pressure)

**#### Bad: unbounded broker-side buffering**
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** if a reader is slow, just let unread data pile up forever and hope it catches up.
- **Approach:** brokers retain everything; slow consumers just have high lag.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** lag is *fine* up to retention — but a consumer stalled longer than `retention_ms` has its un-read data **deleted out from under it** (data loss for that consumer), and a producer pushing faster than disk can absorb fills the page cache and stalls *all* consumers. Unbounded anything is a latent outage.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need an explicit signal and a bounded response.

**#### Good: consumer-paced pull + lag monitoring**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** unbounded buffering hid the problem until retention deleted data; a *pull* model plus lag alarms makes slowness visible and self-limiting.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** consumers ask for data at their own pace instead of being firehosed, and we alarm when any reader falls too far behind.
- **Approach:** consumption is **pull-based** — a slow consumer simply fetches less often, so it can never be overwhelmed; the broker isn't pushing. We alarm on **consumer lag** (high-water mark minus committed offset) per group.
- ⚠️ **What breaks:** pull protects the *consumer*, but a *producer* outrunning total disk/network throughput still has nowhere for its data to go — and lag alarms are reactive, firing after the consumer is already hours behind, with retention as a hard deadline.
- 🔁 **Forces upgrade:** we need to back-pressure the *producer* and shed load *before* retention bites.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Prod[Producer pushes fast]
    Prod -->|"append"| Log[[Partition log retention bound]]
    Slow[Slow consumer pulls at own pace] -->|"fetch when ready"| Log
    Log -.->|"lag alarm fires"| Alert[On call paged]
```

**#### Great: bounded queues + producer quotas + tiered load-shedding**
- ↩️ **What the previous tier got wrong:** pull protected consumers but not the system; quotas and bounded buffers push back on the *producer* and shed in a deliberate order before retention deletes data.
- 🗣️ **Plain words:** cap how fast each producer can push, make its send buffer finite so it blocks (or drops) when full, and if the whole cluster is overloaded, throttle the least-important traffic first to protect the critical topics.
- **Approach:** (1) **producer quotas** — per-client byte-rate limits; over-quota producers get throttled (the broker delays their responses, applying back-pressure up the stack). (2) **Bounded producer buffer** — the client's send buffer is finite; when full it either *blocks* the calling thread or *drops* with an error (configurable) — never unbounded memory growth. (3) **Tiered shedding under cluster overload** — shed/throttle low-priority topics (debug logs, metrics) first; protect the money/CDC topics last. **Trigger signal:** broker request-queue depth + per-partition produce latency p99 breach. (4) **Tiered storage** extends the retention deadline cheaply so a lagging consumer has hours more runway.
- 🔢 **Decision-forcing math:** a hot topic at 100 MB/s with 7-day retention buys a stalled consumer **~168 hours** of runway before data is deleted — but only if producers are quota-capped so they can't fill the disk faster than retention frees it. Without quotas, a runaway producer at 3× burst exhausts that buffer in ~56 hours.
- ✅ **Failure matrix:**

| Scenario | What happens |
|---|---|
| One consumer stalls | its lag grows; alarm fires; it has ~168h of retention runway; others unaffected (pull) |
| Producer exceeds quota | broker throttles its responses; producer's bounded buffer back-pressures the app |
| Producer buffer full | blocks the app thread (or drops with error) — never unbounded memory |
| Cluster-wide overload | shed debug/metrics topics first; protect money/CDC; trigger on queue depth + p99 |
| Consumer lag past retention | un-read data deleted for that consumer (accepted loss for low-tier topics; alarmed early for high-tier) |

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Prod[Producer over quota]
    Prod -->|"1 send"| QChk[Broker quota check]
    QChk -->|"2 throttle response"| Prod
    QChk -->|"3 within quota append"| Log[[Partition log]]
    Buf[Producer bounded buffer] -.->|"4 block app when full"| App[Producer app thread]
    Overload[Cluster overload signal] -->|"5 shed low priority topics first"| Shed[Throttle debug and metrics]
    Log -.->|"6 tier old segments"| Cold[(Object store cold tier)]
```

> 🎙️ **Script:** "Slow consumers can't hurt producers directly because consumption is pull-based — a slow reader just fetches less, the broker never firehoses it. The real risk is a producer outrunning the cluster, so I cap each producer with a byte-rate quota and give it a *bounded* send buffer that blocks or drops when full — never unbounded memory, which is a latent outage. Under cluster-wide overload I shed in priority order: throttle debug and metrics topics first, protect money and CDC last, triggered on broker queue depth and produce-latency p99. And tiered storage buys a lagging consumer extra hours of runway by pushing old segments to cheap object storage. The principle is: every buffer is bounded, and I always protect the correctness path."

> 🧠 **If they ask "what's the trigger to shed load?":** "Broker request-queue depth crossing a threshold plus a produce-latency p99 breach — both leading indicators, so I shed before consumers fall past retention, not after."

## 9. Reliability, Failure Modes & Cost

### 9A — Availability targets (what the nines mean)

- **Write/produce path: 99.95%** — about **4.4 hours/year** of disruption. Mechanisms: RF=3 across **multi-AZ** (three separate data-centre buildings in one region, so losing a building doesn't lose the partition), ISR-based failover (seconds), and `min.insync.replicas=2` so a single AZ loss never loses acked data.
- **Read/consume path: 99.99%** — about **52 minutes/year**. Reads can be served by any ISR replica, and consumers tolerate brief leader failover by retrying from committed offset, so the read path is more available than the write path.
- **Metadata/controller: 99.99%** via a Raft quorum (odd-numbered voting group) across AZs — survives one AZ loss with a majority intact.

### 9B — Per-dependency failure table

| Component | What breaks if it's down | How it degrades gracefully (what the user sees) | Recovery action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Partition leader | writes to that partition pause | producer retries; controller promotes an ISR follower in ~seconds; brief latency blip, no loss | automatic leader election from ISR |
| A follower replica | durability margin shrinks (ISR=2) | invisible to users; writes continue | follower re-syncs from leader on return |
| Whole AZ | ~1/3 of leaders and replicas lost | controller re-elects leaders in surviving AZs; brief write pause, no acked-data loss | leaders rebalance across remaining 2 AZs |
| Controller/metadata quorum | no new elections, config changes freeze | existing leaders keep serving; new failovers can't complete until quorum returns | Raft re-elects controller on majority side |
| Group coordinator | one group can't rebalance/commit | that group's consumers keep reading current assignment from cache; commits queue | coordinator is offsets-topic leader; re-elected via normal failover |
| Object store (cold tier) | replays of old data fail | hot-tail reads unaffected; only rare historical replays error | retry; cold tier is independently HA |

### 9C — RPO / RTO per data class

**Plain definitions first:** **RPO (Recovery Point Objective)** = how much data we're OK losing if everything crashes right now — RPO≈0 means we lose nothing because every acked write was already replicated. **RTO (Recovery Time Objective)** = how long until we're serving again after a failure.

| Data class | RPO | RTO | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| `acks=all` topics (money, CDC) | ≈0 | seconds | ISR replication + min.insync=2; failover from in-sync replica |
| `acks=1` topics (logs, metrics) | up to a few records | seconds | leader-only; rare loss accepted for throughput |
| Committed offsets | ≈0 | seconds | replicated compacted topic (RF=3) |
| Metadata/leadership | ≈0 | seconds | Raft majority-commit |
| Cold-tier archives | ≈0 | minutes | object store 11-nines durability; slower retrieval |

A **tested restore** matters: we periodically promote a fresh replica from cold-tier segments + live log in a staging cluster to prove the archive is actually replayable, not just present.

### 9D — Cost breakdown

Top 3 drivers (from `capacity.py`, total ~**$6.7M/mo**):
1. **Cross-AZ network — ~$2.5M/mo (dominant).** Every byte replicated leader→follower and most consume traffic crosses AZ boundaries, billed per GB (~$0.02/GB round). At ~47 GB/s average cross-AZ, this is the single biggest line item — the messaging equivalent of CDN egress.
2. **Storage — ~$2.2M/mo.** 27 PB of replicated NVMe for 7-day retention.
3. **Broker compute — ~$2.0M/mo.** ~1,365 storage-bound brokers.

**Biggest optimization:** **tiered storage + shorter hot retention.** Offloading segments older than 1 day to object storage (~4× cheaper) and rack-aware/follower-fetch reads (consumers and followers prefer a *same-AZ* replica) attack the two top lines at once — follower-fetch-from-closest-replica alone can cut cross-AZ replication and consume egress materially. **Unit economics: ~$0.22 per 1M messages.**

**Back-pressure on the hottest async path:** the produce→replication path — bounded by producer quotas + bounded send buffers + tiered shedding (see §8); trigger = broker queue depth + produce p99. **Observability:** golden signals are **produce/consume latency** (p99), **traffic** (bytes/s, msgs/s per topic), **errors** (NotLeader, ISR-shrink, rejected writes), **saturation** (broker queue depth, disk %, consumer lag). **Burn-rate alert** on the durability SLO (e.g. any partition with ISR < min.insync for > 1 min pages immediately). **Trace-id propagation:** a correlation id rides in the record header from producer → broker → consumer, so one logical event is traceable across the async hop. **Rate-limiting:** producer quotas (token-bucket per client); over-quota clients get throttled responses (the broker analog of HTTP 429 + Retry-After is a delayed response that signals back-off).

> 🎙️ **Script:** "If a broker dies, the user barely notices — a producer retries for a few seconds while the controller promotes an in-sync replica, and nothing acknowledged is lost. Lose a whole building and it's the same story across two AZs. The thing that *does* hurt is a consumer falling behind — it has about a week of runway, and we alarm long before that. On cost, the surprise for most people is that the dominant line is cross-AZ network, not disk and not compute — so the big lever is keeping replication and reads same-AZ and tiering cold data to object storage."

## 10. Trade-off Ledger

**🔀 Decision: At-least-once delivery (with idempotent consumers) vs exactly-once delivery**
1. **What we chose and why:** at-least-once as the default — far higher throughput and lower latency; duplicates are closed at the consumer with idempotent processing.
2. **What we gave up:** consumers *must* be idempotent; a non-idempotent consumer can double-apply.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** like sending a letter with delivery confirmation but accepting the mail might occasionally deliver a duplicate — so the recipient just ignores letters they've already seen.
4. **When this reverses:** if a critical topic's consumers genuinely cannot dedupe (no natural key), turn on transactional exactly-once for *that* topic and eat the throughput cost.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Default at-least-once, dedupe at the consumer; I only pay the exactly-once tax where the consumer truly can't be made idempotent."

**🔀 Decision: ISR + min.insync.replicas vs ack-all-replicas**
1. **What we chose and why:** ISR-based quorum so durability doesn't couple to the slowest replica.
2. **What we gave up:** when the ISR shrinks below min.insync, we *refuse writes* — we trade availability for durability on that write.
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** we'd rather stop accepting mail than accept mail we might lose.
4. **When this reverses:** for a pure throughput topic where rare loss is fine (metrics/logs), drop to `acks=1` and skip the quorum entirely.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Durability over availability on the money topics — if I can't safely store it on two machines, I refuse it; throughput topics opt down to acks=1."

**🔀 Decision: Per-partition ordering vs global ordering**
1. **What we chose and why:** order only within a partition, so thousands of partitions write in parallel.
2. **What we gave up:** no total order across a topic — consumers needing global order must use a single partition (and lose parallelism).
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** every drawer is perfectly ordered, but there's no single order across all drawers.
4. **When this reverses:** if a use case truly needs global total order, use a single-partition topic and accept the ~10 MB/s ceiling.
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Order where it matters — by key, within a partition. Global order means one writer, which means no scale, so I only do it when the use case demands it."

**🔀 Decision: Single-region multi-AZ vs multi-region**
1. **What we chose and why:** multi-AZ gives building-failure survival with zero added write latency.
2. **What we gave up:** a full region outage takes us down; cross-region DR is async (RPO = mirror lag).
3. **🗣️ Plain words:** safe against one building burning down, not against the whole city losing power.
4. **When this reverses:** a regulatory or RTO requirement to survive a region forces synchronous cross-region (latency tax) or async mirroring (small RPO).
5. **🗣️ How to say it:** "Multi-AZ is free durability; multi-region costs either write latency or a few seconds of possible loss — I'd add it only when a region-survival requirement appears."

**CAP / PACELC in one line:** the produce path is **CP** — under a partition we sacrifice availability (refuse writes when ISR < min.insync) to never lose acked data; and **PACELC**: Else (normal operation) we favour **Latency** for `acks=1` topics and **Consistency** for `acks=all` topics — the producer chooses per write.

> 🎙️ **Script:** "The decisions I'd most defend: at-least-once with idempotent consumers, because exactly-once is a throughput tax I only pay where it's truly needed. ISR with a minimum in-sync count, because I'd rather refuse a write than lose one. And per-partition ordering, because global order means a single writer and no scale. The thing that would flip the biggest one — multi-region — is a hard region-survival requirement; until then multi-AZ is durability for free."

## 11. Likely Interviewer Questions & Answers

**❓ "Two producers write to the same key at the same time — what's the order?"**
**The mechanism:** same key → same partition (the partitioner hashes the key), and a partition has a single leader that assigns a strictly increasing offset to each append. So the two writes are serialized by the leader in arrival order; whoever the leader appends first gets the lower offset.
**🗣️ In plain words:** same key always goes to the same drawer, and the drawer has one scribe who stamps a page number on arrival — so there's always a clear "first."
**💬 One-liner:** "Same key, same partition, single leader assigns offsets — order is whatever the leader saw first."

**❓ "What happens to acknowledged data if the leader crashes right after acking?"**
**The mechanism:** with `acks=all` and `min.insync=2`, the ack only went out *after* the ISR (≥2 machines) had the record. The controller elects a new leader *from the ISR*, which by definition has that record. Zero loss.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we only said "got it" once a backup already had a copy, so a backup takes over with the data intact.
**💬 One-liner:** "Acked data is always on the in-sync set, and we only promote from the in-sync set — so a leader crash loses nothing."

**❓ "Can a consumer process the same message twice?"**
**The mechanism:** yes — that's the at-least-once contract. A consumer commits its offset *after* processing; if it crashes in between, it reprocesses on restart. We make this safe with idempotent consumers that dedupe by a natural key in a TTL'd dedupe store.
**🗣️ In plain words:** a crash can make a reader re-read the last message, so readers are built to ignore anything they've already handled.
**💬 One-liner:** "At-least-once means duplicates are possible — we close the window with idempotent processing, not by promising delivery is perfect."

**❓ "A whole AZ goes down — what's the impact?"**
**The mechanism:** RF=3 spreads replicas across 3 AZs, so one AZ loss takes ~1/3 of leaders and replicas. The controller re-elects leaders in the surviving 2 AZs; writes pause for seconds, ISR stays ≥2 for most partitions, no acked data is lost.
**🗣️ In plain words:** lose one building and the other two carry on after a brief blip.
**💬 One-liner:** "Three copies across three buildings means one building can vanish with a few seconds of failover and zero data loss."

**❓ "What if a consumer falls way behind — does it lose data?"**
**The mechanism:** consumer lag (high-water mark minus committed offset) grows but is harmless until it exceeds retention; we alarm well before that, and tiered storage extends the deadline by keeping cold segments cheaply in object storage. A consumer stalled past retention loses its un-read data — accepted only for low-tier topics.
**🗣️ In plain words:** a slow reader has about a week of runway; we page on-call long before the oldest pages get thrown out.
**💬 One-liner:** "Lag is fine up to retention — ~a week of runway — and we alarm early; tiered storage buys more."

**❓ "How do you handle a flash flood of produce traffic — a retry storm?"**
**The mechanism:** producer quotas (per-client byte-rate token buckets) throttle over-budget clients via delayed responses, which back-pressures their bounded send buffers up to the app. Under cluster-wide overload we shed low-priority topics (debug/metrics) first, protecting money/CDC. Trigger = broker queue depth + produce p99.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we cap how fast each app can push, and if the whole cluster is drowning we throttle the least-important traffic first.
**💬 One-liner:** "Bounded buffers plus per-client quotas plus priority load-shedding — never an unbounded queue, and we protect the correctness path."

**❓ "Can the same logical event be published twice by a retrying producer?"**
**The mechanism:** the idempotent producer stamps each record with `(producerId, partition, sequence)`. The leader tracks the last sequence per producer-partition and rejects a duplicate sequence, so a retry after a lost ack is dedup'd server-side.
**🗣️ In plain words:** each send carries a counter, and the broker ignores a counter it's already seen, so a retry can't double-write.
**💬 One-liner:** "Idempotent producer sequence numbers mean a retried publish is silently dropped by the leader — no double-append."

**❓ "How do you stop a malicious or buggy client from reading another team's topic?"**
**The mechanism:** the connection is authenticated via mTLS/SASL (identity is never client-supplied), and per-topic ACLs check this *principal* against PRODUCE/CONSUME permission on *this specific topic* on every connection — object-level authz, not just authn.
**🗣️ In plain words:** we check both *who you are* and *whether you're allowed to touch this specific topic*, every time.
**💬 One-liner:** "mTLS identity plus per-topic ACLs — authn answers 'who', authz answers 'allowed on THIS topic'."

**❓ "What's the most expensive part, and how would you cut it?"**
**The mechanism:** cross-AZ network (~$2.5M/mo) dominates — replication and consume traffic crossing AZ boundaries, billed per GB. The fix is rack/AZ-aware placement plus follower-fetch and consumer-fetch from the *closest* (same-AZ) replica, plus tiering cold data to object storage (~4× cheaper than NVMe).
**🗣️ In plain words:** the biggest bill is moving bytes between buildings, so we keep reads and replication within a building wherever we can.
**💬 One-liner:** "Cross-AZ traffic is the CDN-egress of messaging — same-AZ fetch and tiered storage are the big levers."

**❓ "Can you oversell partition ordering — i.e. can records for one key end up out of order?"**
**The mechanism:** only if you (a) increase partition count (changes key→partition mapping for new writes) or (b) run with `max.in.flight > 1` *without* the idempotent producer (a retry can reorder). With the idempotent producer, in-flight requests are sequenced, so order holds even with retries.
**🗣️ In plain words:** order is safe as long as you don't reshuffle partitions mid-stream and you turn on the idempotent producer.
**💬 One-liner:** "Per-key order holds unless you repartition or disable idempotency — both are explicit, avoidable choices."

**❓ "How would you add true exactly-once across consume-process-produce later?"**
**The mechanism:** add a **transaction coordinator** — the consumer reads, processes, produces output *and* commits its input offset inside a single broker transaction; consumers downstream read with `read_committed` so they only see committed output. It reuses the existing log + a transaction-state topic.
**🗣️ In plain words:** wrap "read, transform, write, bookmark" in one all-or-nothing transaction so a crash can't leave it half-done.
**💬 One-liner:** "A transaction coordinator makes consume-process-produce atomic — opt-in per pipeline because it costs throughput."

**❓ "How would you support multi-region / disaster recovery?"**
**The mechanism:** async mirroring (a MirrorMaker-style consumer reads the source cluster and produces into a remote cluster) gives RPO = mirror lag (seconds) with no write-latency tax; or synchronous cross-region `acks=all` for RPO≈0 at the cost of cross-region latency on every write.
**🗣️ In plain words:** either copy data to another region a few seconds behind, or write to both regions before acking — speed vs zero-loss.
**💬 One-liner:** "Async mirror for cheap DR with seconds of RPO, or synchronous cross-region writes for zero loss and a latency tax — pick per the requirement."

**❓ "Why a log instead of a traditional message queue?"**
**The mechanism:** a log decouples retention from consumption — multiple independent consumer groups read the same data at their own pace, and any can replay by resetting its offset. A queue deletes on ack, so there's one consumer's worth of data and no replay.
**🗣️ In plain words:** the log keeps a week of history that many readers can re-read independently; a queue throws each message away once one reader handles it.
**💬 One-liner:** "A log gives replay and many independent readers; a queue gives point-to-point delivery and forgets — different jobs."

> 🎙️ **60-second verbal summary:** "This is a durable, replayable log: producers append events to partitioned topics, consumers read them by offset and can rewind. The design splits into two halves. The read half is consumer groups — a coordinator hands each consumer a slice of partitions, they pull records at their own pace and bookmark their position in an internal compacted offsets topic, and cooperative rebalancing reassigns only what must move when membership changes. The write half is partitioned, replicated append-only logs — each partition has a single leader for ordering and an in-sync replica set for durability; the hardest problem is never losing an acknowledged write, and the mechanism that solves it is the ISR with a minimum in-sync count, so we only acknowledge data that's safely on two machines and only ever fail over to a replica that already has it. The two halves connect through the partition log itself — the same append-only stream that producers write, followers replicate, and consumers replay. And every hard decision traces back to one principle: never lose an acknowledged write and never violate per-partition order, and trade everything else — latency, availability, even throughput — to protect those two."
